Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code's tokens being doubled during non-working hours in 2026:

## Claude March 2026 Usage Promotion

From March 13, 2026 through March 28, 2026, your five-hour usage is doubled during off-peak hours (outside 8 AM-2 PM ET / 5-11 AM PT / 12-6 PM GMT) on weekdays.

### Key Details:

**When it applies:**
- Off-peak hours are anytime outside of 8am to 2pm Eastern Time, which translates to 5am to 11am Pacific Time on weekdays
- On weekends, the doubled limits apply around the clock

**What's included:**
- This applies to Claude (web, desktop, mobile), Claude Code, Cowork, Claude for Excel, and Claude for PowerPoint
- It's available for Free, Pro, Max, and Team plans
- The only users that won't get to take advantage of this are Enterprise plan subscribers

**Important points:**
- The extra usage does not count toward weekly caps, which means it is genuinely additive, not a reshuffling of existing limits
- No enrollment required. If you're on an eligible plan, the doubled limits apply automatically

The promotion ends on March 27, 2026.

Yes, it's real. Anthropic is running a promotion **March 13–27, 2026** that doubles usage limits during **off-peak hours**.

**Off-peak = outside 8 AM – 2 PM ET on weekdays. Weekends: all day.**

Applies to Claude Code, all plan tiers except Enterprise. No enrollment needed — automatic.

Today is March 22 (Sunday), so you're getting 2x all day. Ends March 27.

Sources:
- [Claude March 2026 Usage Promotion | Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/14063676-claude-march-2026-usage-promotion)
- [XDA Developers](https://www.xda-developers.com/claude-doubled-every-users-usage-limits-for-two-weeks/)
- [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/ai/anthropic-is-doubling-claudes-usage-limits-during-off-peak-hours-for-the-next-two-weeks-163645928.html)
- [Tom's Guide](https://www.tomsguide.com/ai/a-small-thank-you-to-users-claude-extends-usage-limits-for-every-user-but-theres-a-slight-catch)

Off-peak for you (CEST = UTC+2):

**Weekdays:** before 2 PM CEST or after 8 PM CEST
**Weekends:** all day

So most of your working day is actually off-peak. Good timing.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/4thwithme/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.5/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** — dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 3 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

content.content:
1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Tools & Requirements
     6→
     7→- **Node.js**: >=v24.13.0 (see `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`)
     8→- **npm**: >=11.0.0
     9→- **Core stack**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript (strict mode)
    10→- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
    11→- **Code quality**: ESLint v9+ (flat config), Prettier, Husky
    12→
    13→## Key Commands
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Development
    17→npm run start:dev          # Hot reload via webpack
    18→npm run start:debug        # Debug mode with hot reload
    19→npm run build              # Production build
    20→npm run start:prod         # Run production build
    21→
    22→# Testing
    23→npm run test               # Full suite (lint, format, type-check, unit, e2e)
    24→npm run test:unit          # Unit tests only
    25→npm run test:e2e           # E2E tests only
    26→npm run test:coverage      # Full suite with coverage reports
    27→npm run test:unit:coverage # Unit tests with coverage
    28→npm run test:e2e:coverage  # E2E tests with coverage
    29→
    30→# Code Quality
    31→npm run code-quality-check # lint + format + type-check
    32→npm run lint               # Check linting
    33→npm run lint:fix           # Auto-fix linting issues
    34→npm run format             # Prettier format
    35→npm run type-check         # TypeScript check without emit
    36→```
    37→
    38→## Architecture
    39→
    40→NestJS microservice with Fastify adapter, strict TypeScript, and modular domain organization.
    41→
    42→### Project Structure
    43→
    44→```
    45→src/
    46→├── modules/
    47→│   ├── app/              # Root application module
    48→│   ├── health/           # Health check endpoints (liveness + readiness)
    49→│   └── env-config/       # Environment configuration & validation
    50→├── configs/              # Configuration files
    51→│   └── env-validations.config.ts  # class-validator decorators for env vars
    52→├── constants/            # Constants and enums
    53→│   └── env-variables.constant.ts  # Env variable name string exports
    54→├── dto/                  # Data Transfer Objects
    55→├── utils/                # Utility functions
    56→│   ├── common.util.ts           # Environment detection helpers
    57→│   ├── get-env-file.util.ts     # Env file loader & validator
    58→│   ├── get-env-variable.util.ts # getEnv standalone helper
    59→│   └── setup-swagger.util.ts    # Swagger/OpenAPI setup
    60→├── middlewares/          # Custom middlewares
    61→├── interfaces/           # TypeScript interfaces
    62→├── decorators/           # Custom decorators
    63→├── interceptors/         # NestJS interceptors
    64→├── guards/               # Auth guards
    65→└── unit-tests/           # Unit tests (*.unit-spec.ts)
    66→test/                     # E2E tests (*.e2e-spec.ts)
    67→eslint-rules/             # Custom ESLint rules
    68→└── require-object-params.js  # Enforces single object param rule
    69→```
    70→
    71→### Import Aliases (tsconfig.json)
    72→
    73→```typescript
    74→@config/*        → src/configs/*
    75→@constants/*     → src/constants/*
    76→@modules/*       → src/modules/*
    77→@utils/*         → src/utils/*
    78→@middlewares/*   → src/middlewares/*
    79→@services/*      → src/services/*
    80→@interfaces/*    → src/interfaces/*
    81→@decorators/*    → src/decorators/*
    82→@dto/*           → src/dto/*
    83→@guards/*        → src/guards/*
    84→@interceptors/*  → src/interceptors/*
    85→```
    86→
    87→## Development Rules
    88→
    89→### Code Style
    90→
    91→- **NO COMMENTS**, **NO EXPLANATIONS** in code (except ESLint disable commands)
    92→- **ALWAYS INSPECT EXISTING FILES** before creating new ones — match exact patterns:
    93→  - `.service.ts` → check similar modules
    94→  - `.controller.ts` → check `/src/modules/`
    95→  - `.dto.ts` → check `/src/dto/` or module folders
    96→  - `.util.ts` → check `/src/utils/`
    97→  - `.unit-spec.ts` → check `/src/unit-tests/`
    98→  - `.e2e-spec.ts` → check `/test/`
    99→  - `.interface.ts` → check `/src/interfaces/`
   100→- No `console.log` — use NestJS `Logger` instead
   101→- No relative imports — use `@aliases` only
   102→- All functions must have explicit return types
   103→- All custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter (enforced by custom ESLint rule `require-object-params`)
   104→
   105→### Environment Variables (CRITICAL)
   106→
   107→**ALWAYS follow this complete process when adding new environment variables:**
   108→
   109→#### Step 1: Add to Environment Files
   110→
   111→Add to all four env files:
   112→
   113→```bash
   114→# .env, .env.example
   115→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=default_value   # Description of what this variable does
   116→```
   117→
   118→```bash
   119→# .env.test, .env.test.example
   120→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=test_value
   121→```
   122→
   123→#### Step 2: Create Constant Export
   124→
   125→Add to `src/constants/env-variables.constant.ts`:
   126→
   127→```typescript
   128→export const NEW_VARIABLE_NAME = 'NEW_VARIABLE_NAME';
   129→```
   130→
   131→#### Step 3: Add Validation Rule
   132→
   133→Add to `src/configs/env-validations.config.ts` in the `EnvironmentVariables` class:
   134→
   135→```typescript
   136→@IsString()    // Use appropriate validator
   137→@IsNotEmpty()
   138→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: string;
   139→```
   140→
   141→**Common validators:** `@IsString()`, `@IsNumber()`, `@IsBoolean()`, `@IsEnum(EnumType)`, `@IsNotEmpty()`, `@Min()`, `@Max()`, `@IsUrl()`
   142→
   143→#### Step 4: Add to Environment Loader
   144→
   145→Add to `src/utils/get-env-file.util.ts` in `getEnvVariables()`:
   146→
   147→```typescript
   148→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: process.env['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
   149→```
   150→
   151→#### Step 5: Add Test Coverage
   152→
   153→Add to `test/env-config.module.e2e-spec.ts`:
   154→
   155→```typescript
   156→// In beforeAll hook
   157→testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'] = String(envConfig['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME']);
   158→
   159→// Test case
   160→it('should load NEW_VARIABLE_NAME correctly', () => {
   161→  expect(envConfigService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME })).toBe(
   162→    testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
   163→  );
   164→});
   165→```
   166→
   167→#### Usage: Two Approaches
   168→
   169→**Approach 1: EnvConfigService (Dependency Injection)**
   170→
   171→```typescript
   172→import { EnvConfigService } from '@modules/env-config/env-config.service';
   173→import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';
   174→
   175→@Injectable()
   176→export class MyService {
   177→  constructor(private readonly configService: EnvConfigService) {}
   178→
   179→  someMethod(): void {
   180→    const value = this.configService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
   181→    const numericValue = this.configService.get<number>({
   182→      key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME,
   183→      converter: Number,
   184→    });
   185→  }
   186→}
   187→```
   188→
   189→**Approach 2: getEnv Utility (Standalone)**
   190→
   191→```typescript
   192→import { getEnv } from '@utils/get-env-variable.util';
   193→import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';
   194→
   195→const value = getEnv<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
   196→const numericValue = getEnv<number>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME, converter: Number });
   197→```
   198→
   199→**Important:**
   200→- **ALWAYS** complete ALL 5 steps — missing any step causes validation failures
   201→- **NEVER** access `process.env` directly in application code
   202→- **ALWAYS** use constants from `@constants/env-variables.constant`
   203→
   204→### Logging
   205→
   206→```typescript
   207→import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
   208→
   209→export class ClassName {
   210→  private readonly logger = new Logger(ClassName.name);
   211→}
   212→```
   213→
   214→### Common Patterns
   215→
   216→```typescript
   217→// Swagger decorators on controllers
   218→@ApiOperation({ summary: 'Description' })
   219→@ApiResponse({ status: HttpStatus.OK, description: 'Success' })
   220→```
   221→
   222→## ESLint Configuration
   223→
   224→- **No relative imports** (`no-restricted-imports` with `.*` pattern)
   225→- **No console** — use Logger
   226→- **Explicit return types** on all functions (`@typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types`)
   227→- **Import order**: builtin → external → internal (`@aliases`) → type, alphabetized, newline between groups
   228→- **Custom rule** `require-object-params`: all custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter
   229→- **Relaxed rules** in `*.spec.ts`, `test/**`, `src/decorators/**`, `src/middlewares/**`, `src/interceptors/**`, `src/guards/**`
   230→
   231→## TypeScript Configuration
   232→
   233→- Target ES2023, strict mode with all checks enabled
   234→- `strictPropertyInitialization: false` (NestJS DI pattern)
   235→- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, `noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature` enabled — be precise with types
   236→- Incremental compilation enabled for faster builds
   237→- Separate `tsconfig.test.json` relaxes strict checks for test files
   238→
   239→## Testing
   240→
   241→- **Unit**: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
   242→- **E2E**: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
   243→- **Coverage**: Separate reports in `coverage-unit/` and `coverage-e2e/`
   244→- Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
   245→- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements
   246→
   247→### Jest Config Files
   248→
   249→```
   250→test/
   251→├── jest.config.ts              # Shared base (moduleNameMapper, transform, preset)
   252→├── jest.coverage.config.ts     # Base + 95% thresholds
   253→├── jest-unit.config.ts         # Unit runner (testRegex: unit-spec.ts)
   254→├── jest-unit.coverage.config.ts # Unit + coverage enforcement
   255→├── jest-e2e.config.ts          # E2E runner (testRegex: e2e-spec.ts)
   256→└── jest-e2e.coverage.config.ts  # E2E + coverage enforcement (controllers + services only)
   257→```
   258→
   259→### Unit Test Patterns
   260→
   261→```typescript
   262→// Service test — mock dependencies via Test.createTestingModule
   263→const module: TestingModule = await Test.createTestingModule({
   264→  providers: [
   265→    MyService,
   266→    { provide: MyDependency, useValue: mockDependency },
   267→  ],
   268→}).compile();
   269→service = module.get<MyService>(MyService);
   270→
   271→// Spy on Logger to prevent console noise and assert calls
   272→loggerSpy = jest.spyOn(Logger.prototype, 'log').mockImplementation(jest.fn());
   273→
   274→// Always clear mocks between tests
   275→afterEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks());
   276→```
   277→
   278→### E2E Test Patterns
   279→
   280→```typescript
   281→// HTTP testing — let TerminusModule run the real health check chain
   282→const moduleFixture = await Test.createTestingModule({
   283→  imports: [TerminusModule.forRoot({ gracefulShutdownTimeoutMs: 0, errorLogStyle: 'json' })],
   284→  controllers: [MyController],
   285→  providers: [MyService, { provide: Dependency, useValue: mock }],
   286→}).compile();
   287→
   288→const app = moduleFixture.createNestApplication();
   289→const httpServer = app.getHttpServer() as App;
   290→await app.init();
   291→
   292→// Use supertest for HTTP assertions
   293→await request(httpServer).get('/endpoint').expect(200);
   294→```
   295→
   296→### Coverage Requirements
   297→
   298→- **95% minimum** on branches, functions, lines, statements — enforced by Jest, CI fails if not met
   299→- Unit coverage collects from all `src/**/*.ts` (excluding `main.ts`, modules, interfaces, constants, test files)
   300→- E2E coverage collects only from `*.(controller|service).ts` files
   301→
   302→## Key Dependencies
   303→
   304→### Core
   305→
   306→- **@nestjs/\*** — NestJS framework and modules
   307→- **@nestjs/platform-fastify** — Fastify adapter for performance
   308→- **@nestjs/terminus** — Health check module
   309→- **@nestjs/swagger** — OpenAPI/Swagger documentation
   310→
   311→### Validation & Transform
   312→
   313→- **class-validator** + **class-transformer** — DTO validation and transformation
   314→
   315→### Utilities
   316→
   317→- **rxjs** — Reactive patterns
   318→- **axios** / **@nestjs/axios** — HTTP requests
   319→

Perfect. Let me compile my findings into a comprehensive report.

## Authentication/Authorization Exploration Report

Based on my thorough exploration of this NestJS + React monorepo, here's what exists and what's missing:

### WHAT EXISTS

**1. Backend Project Foundation**
- NestJS v11 + Fastify adapter (strict TypeScript mode)
- Well-organized modular structure with proper import aliases
- Environment configuration system in place (EnvConfigService, env validations)
- Health check endpoints (liveness/readiness via Terminus)
- Swagger/OpenAPI setup utilities
- Global validation pipe with class-validator
- CORS enabled (origin: true, credentials: true)

**2. Project Directories (Empty but Planned)**
- `/backend/src/guards/` - prepared directory for auth guards (.gitkeep)
- `/backend/src/decorators/` - prepared directory for custom decorators (.gitkeep)
- `/backend/src/middlewares/` - prepared directory for middlewares (.gitkeep)
- `/backend/src/dto/` - prepared directory for DTOs (.gitkeep)

**3. Development Infrastructure**
- Jest testing (unit + e2E) with 95% coverage requirements
- ESLint v9 with custom rules (no relative imports, no console.log)
- Prettier formatting
- Husky pre-commit hooks
- Comprehensive env variable validation system

### WHAT'S MISSING

**1. Core Authentication Libraries**
- No passport, passport-jwt, passport-local, or other strategies
- No @nestjs/jwt or jwt-decode
- No OAuth/OIDC libraries (passport-google-oauth20, etc.)
- No bcrypt or password hashing libraries
- No @nestjs/passport package

**2. Database/ORM**
- No TypeORM, Prisma, Drizzle, or any ORM installed
- No database connection setup
- No user entity/model defined
- No migrations system

**3. Authentication-Related Environment Variables**
- `.env.example` contains only basic app config (PORT, NODE_ENV, etc.)
- Missing: JWT_SECRET, JWT_EXPIRY, DB_URL, OAUTH providers, session config, etc.

**4. Authentication Modules & Services**
- No auth module
- No user module/service
- No auth controller
- No JWT strategy
- No session/token management service
- No password reset/email verification flow

**5. Security Features**
- No rate limiting (@nestjs/throttler or express-rate-limit)
- No helmet for HTTP headers
- No request validation for auth endpoints
- No logout/token blacklist system
- No refresh token implementation

**6. Frontend**
- Frontend directory exists but appears empty (scaffolding not completed per CLAUDE.md)
- No frontend package.json or auth context/hooks

### FILE STRUCTURE REFERENCES

**Existing modules:**
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/src/modules/app/app.module.ts`
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/src/modules/env-config/` (env configuration)
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/src/modules/health/` (health checks)

**Configuration files:**
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/src/configs/env-validations.config.ts` (env validation template)
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/.env.example` (basic env vars only)

**Package.json dependencies:**
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/package.json` (no auth packages present)

### KEY OBSERVATIONS

1. **Ready for Auth Implementation** - The project has excellent scaffolding and conventions that will make adding auth straightforward
2. **Environment System Ready** - The 5-step env variable process is already established and can accommodate auth secrets/tokens
3. **No Database Yet** - You'll need to choose and configure an ORM before implementing user models
4. **Architecture Pattern Clear** - The modular approach (modules/services/controllers/DTOs) is well-defined and documented in `/backend/CLAUDE.md`

## Authentication/Authorization Exploration Report

Based on my thorough exploration of this NestJS + React monorepo, here's what exists and what's missing:

### WHAT EXISTS

**2. Project Directories (Empty but Planned)**

- `/backend/src/decorators/` - prepared directory for custom decorators (.gitkeep)
- `/backend/src/middlewares/` - prepared directory for middlewares (.gitkeep)
- `/backend/src/dto/` - prepared directory for DTOs (.gitkeep)

**3. Development Infrastructure**
- Jest testing (unit + e2E) with 95% coverage requirements
- ESLint v9 with custom rules (no relative imports, no console.log)
- Prettier formatting
- Husky pre-commit hooks
- Comprehensive env variable validation system

### WHAT'S MISSING

**1. Core Authentication Libraries**
- No passport, passport-jwt, passport-local, or other strategies
- No @nestjs/jwt or jwt-decode
- No OAuth/OIDC libraries (passport-google-oauth20, etc.)
- No bcrypt or password hashing libraries
- No @nestjs/passport package

**2. Database/ORM**
- No TypeORM, Prisma, Drizzle, or any ORM installed
- No database connection setup
- No user entity/model defined
- No migrations system

**3. Authentication-Related Environment Variables**
- `.env.example` contains only basic app config (PORT, NODE_ENV, etc.)
- Missing: JWT_SECRET, JWT_EXPIRY, DB_URL, OAUTH providers, session config, etc.

**4. Authentication Modules & Services**
- No auth module
- No user module/service
- No auth controller
- No JWT strategy
- No session/token management service
- No password reset/email verification flow

**5. Security Features**
- No rate limiting (@nestjs/throttler or express-rate-limit)
- No helmet for HTTP headers
- No request validation for auth endpoints
- No logout/token blacklist system
- No refresh token implementation

**6. Frontend**
- Frontend directory exists but appears empty (scaffolding not completed per CLAUDE.md)
- No frontend package.json or auth context/hooks

### FILE STRUCTURE REFERENCES

**Existing modules:**
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/src/modules/app/app.module.ts`

content.text:
Project state: NestJS + Fastify backend with good scaffolding but **no database, no ORM, no auth libraries yet**. Clean slate for auth.

Before I propose approaches, first question:

**Do you need a database for user persistence, or is this purely OAuth-based (stateless)?**

Options:
- **A) Full user model** — Store users in a DB, link OAuth accounts, support profile data, roles, etc.
- **B) Stateless OAuth** — Just verify the OAuth token on each request, no local user storage
- **C) Lightweight hybrid** — Minimal user record in DB (id, email, provider) just to track who's logged in, no rich profile

Which fits your use case?

content.text:
Good context. So we're building a multi-tenant B2B app with company-scoped data.

Next question:

**Which database do you want to use?**

Options:
- **A) PostgreSQL** — Best fit for relational multi-tenant data (companies, users, roles). Most common with NestJS
- **B) MongoDB** — Document-based, more flexible schema but weaker for relational queries
- **C) No preference** — I'll recommend based on your use case

message.content: lets use postgresql with read and write replicas

content.text:
Good choice. Read/write replicas from the start — that tells me you're planning for scale.

Next question:

**Which ORM/query layer?**

Options:
- **A) Prisma** — Type-safe, great DX, schema-first migrations. Some limitations with read replicas (needs `@prisma/extension-read-replicas` or manual datasource switching)
- **B) TypeORM** — Native NestJS integration, decorator-based entities, built-in replication config. More mature but weaker type safety
- **C) Drizzle** — Lightweight, SQL-like syntax, excellent TypeScript types, easy multi-datasource setup. Newer but gaining traction fast
- **D) No preference**

My lean: **Drizzle or TypeORM**. Both handle read/write replicas natively without extensions. Prisma's replica support is bolted on and adds complexity.

1→# DB Modules Guide
     2→
     3→This directory contains all database models and services for MySQL (via Knex.js) and Redshift (PostgreSQL). Each sub-directory is a self-contained domain module.
     4→
     5→## Read/Write Connection Pattern
     6→
     7→Every model injects both connections. Use `knexRead` for all SELECT queries and `knexWrite` for all INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations and transactions.
     8→
     9→```typescript
    10→import { Knex } from 'knex';
    11→import { InjectConnection } from 'nest-knexjs';
    12→import { KNEX_READ_CONNECTION, KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION } from '@constants/db.constant';
    13→
    14→constructor(
    15→  @InjectConnection(KNEX_READ_CONNECTION) private readonly knexRead: Knex,
    16→  @InjectConnection(KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION) private readonly knexWrite: Knex,
    17→) {}
    18→```
    19→
    20→Redshift modules use `REDSHIFT_READ_CONNECTION` (PostgreSQL) and only need a read connection.
    21→
    22→## Module Structure
    23→
    24→Each domain module follows this layout:
    25→
    26→```
    27→<domain>/
    28→├── models/
    29→│   └── <domain>.model.ts        # Knex queries, DTO validation, logging
    30→├── types/
    31→│   ├── <domain>.base.interface.ts   # Full row shape
    32→│   ├── <domain>.create.interface.ts # Omit<IBase, '_id' | timestamps>
    33→│   └── <domain>.update.interface.ts # Partial<Omit<IBase, '_id' | timestamps>>
    34→├── dto/
    35→│   ├── <domain>.base.dto.ts     # class-validator decorators on all fields
    36→│   ├── <domain>.create.dto.ts   # OmitType(BaseDto, ['_id', 'created_at', 'updated_at', 'deleted_at'])
    37→│   └── <domain>.update.dto.ts   # PartialType(OmitType(BaseDto, [...]))
    38→└── <domain>.service.ts          # Thin facade over the model, @LogExecution where needed
    39→```
    40→
    41→Redshift modules omit the `dto/` layer and do not have soft deletes.
    42→
    43→## Interface Conventions
    44→
    45→```typescript
    46→// types/<domain>.base.interface.ts
    47→export interface IDomainBase {
    48→  _id: string;           // UUID primary key
    49→  // ... domain fields
    50→  created_at: Date;
    51→  updated_at: Date;
    52→  deleted_at: Date | null;
    53→}
    54→
    55→// types/<domain>.create.interface.ts
    56→export type IDomainCreate = Omit<IDomainBase, '_id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at' | 'deleted_at'>;
    57→
    58→// types/<domain>.update.interface.ts
    59→export type IDomainUpdate = Partial<Omit<IDomainBase, '_id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at' | 'deleted_at'>>;
    60→```
    61→
    62→## DTO Conventions
    63→
    64→Base DTO uses class-validator decorators on every property. `@IsOptional()` precedes the type validator for nullable fields.
    65→
    66→```typescript
    67→// dto/<domain>.base.dto.ts
    68→import { IsInt, IsNotEmpty, IsOptional, IsString, IsUUID } from 'class-validator';
    69→
    70→export class BaseDomainDto {
    71→  @IsUUID()
    72→  @IsNotEmpty()
    73→  _id: string;
    74→
    75→  @IsInt()
    76→  @IsNotEmpty()
    77→  style_id: number;
    78→
    79→  @IsOptional()
    80→  @IsString()
    81→  some_nullable_field: string | null;
    82→
    83→  @IsNotEmpty()
    84→  created_at: Date;
    85→
    86→  @IsNotEmpty()
    87→  updated_at: Date;
    88→
    89→  @IsOptional()
    90→  deleted_at: Date | null;
    91→}
    92→
    93→// dto/<domain>.create.dto.ts
    94→import { OmitType } from '@nestjs/swagger';
    95→import { BaseDomainDto } from './domain.base.dto';
    96→
    97→export class CreateDomainDto extends OmitType(BaseDomainDto, [
    98→  '_id', 'created_at', 'updated_at', 'deleted_at',
    99→] as const) {}
   100→
   101→// dto/<domain>.update.dto.ts
   102→import { PartialType, OmitType } from '@nestjs/swagger';
   103→import { BaseDomainDto } from './domain.base.dto';
   104→
   105→export class UpdateDomainDto extends PartialType(
   106→  OmitType(BaseDomainDto, ['_id', 'created_at', 'updated_at', 'deleted_at'] as const),
   107→) {}
   108→```
   109→
   110→Use `@Transform` from `class-transformer` when the DB stores a value in a different format (e.g., JSON columns stored as strings, tinyint booleans).
   111→
   112→## Model Conventions
   113→
   114→Every model class follows this structure:
   115→
   116→```typescript
   117→import { randomUUID } from 'node:crypto';
   118→import { Injectable, Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
   119→import { instanceToPlain, plainToInstance } from 'class-transformer';
   120→import { validate, ValidationError } from 'class-validator';
   121→import { Knex } from 'knex';
   122→import { InjectConnection } from 'nest-knexjs';
   123→import { KNEX_READ_CONNECTION, KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION, TABLE_NAME_X } from '@constants/db.constant';
   124→
   125→@Injectable()
   126→export class DomainModel {
   127→  private readonly logger = new Logger(DomainModel.name);
   128→  private readonly tableName = TABLE_NAME_X;
   129→
   130→  constructor(
   131→    @InjectConnection(KNEX_READ_CONNECTION) private readonly knexRead: Knex,
   132→    @InjectConnection(KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION) private readonly knexWrite: Knex,
   133→  ) {}
   134→
   135→  // ... methods
   136→}
   137→```
   138→
   139→Key patterns inside methods:
   140→- Validate input DTOs with `plainToInstance` + `validate` before writes.
   141→- Validate output DTOs with `plainToInstance` + `validate` after reads.
   142→- Use `instanceToPlain` when returning data to callers.
   143→- All methods wrap logic in try/catch, log errors with `this.logger.error`, and rethrow.
   144→- Use `this.logger.debug` for success paths and `this.logger.warn` for not-found cases.
   145→- Format validation errors using a private `formatValidationErrors(errors: ValidationError[]): string` helper.
   146→
   147→```typescript
   148→private formatValidationErrors(errors: ValidationError[]): string {
   149→  return errors
   150→    .map((error) => `${error.property}: ${Object.values(error.constraints ?? {}).join(', ')}`)
   151→    .join('; ');
   152→}
   153→```
   154→
   155→## Soft Deletes
   156→
   157→Tables with a `deleted_at` column use soft deletes. Apply these rules consistently:
   158→
   159→- **All reads**: always chain `.whereNull('deleted_at')`.
   160→- **Soft delete**: set `deleted_at` and `updated_at` to `knexWrite.fn.now()`, never hard-delete.
   161→- **Never** hard delete rows from soft-delete tables.
   162→
   163→```typescript
   164→// Read — always filter soft-deleted rows
   165→await this.knexRead(this.tableName)
   166→  .where({ style_id: styleId })
   167→  .whereNull('deleted_at')
   168→  .first();
   169→
   170→// Soft delete
   171→await this.knexWrite(this.tableName)
   172→  .where({ style_id: styleId })
   173→  .whereNull('deleted_at')
   174→  .update({
   175→    deleted_at: this.knexWrite.fn.now(),
   176→    updated_at: this.knexWrite.fn.now(),
   177→  });
   178→```
   179→
   180→Tables without `deleted_at` in their schema (e.g., some Redshift tables) do not use soft deletes.
   181→
   182→## Transactions
   183→
   184→Use `knexWrite.transaction` for operations that must be atomic. Pass the `trx` object in place of `knexWrite` inside the callback.
   185→
   186→```typescript
   187→await this.knexWrite.transaction(async (trx) => {
   188→  await trx(this.tableName).insert(insertData);
   189→  const result = await trx(this.tableName).where('_id', _id).whereNull('deleted_at').first();
   190→  // ...
   191→});
   192→```
   193→
   194→For operations that accept an external transaction (e.g., coordinating across models), accept `trx: Knex.Transaction` as a parameter rather than creating a new one.
   195→
   196→## Inserting Rows
   197→
   198→Always generate the primary key in application code using `randomUUID()` from `node:crypto`.
   199→
   200→```typescript
   201→import { randomUUID } from 'node:crypto';
   202→
   203→const _id = randomUUID();
   204→await trx(this.tableName).insert({
   205→  _id,
   206→  ...data,
   207→  created_at: trx.fn.now(),
   208→  updated_at: trx.fn.now(),
   209→});
   210→```
   211→
   212→## Table Name Constants
   213→
   214→All table names are exported from `@constants/db.constant`. Never use raw string literals for table names.
   215→
   216→| Constant | Table | Module |
   217→|---|---|---|
   218→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLES` | `styles` | `styles` |
   219→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLES_COLORS` | `styles_colors` | `styles-colors` |
   220→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLES_HASH` | `styles_hash` | `styles-hash` |
   221→| `TABLE_NAME_CONDENSED_FEATURES` | `condensed_features` | `condensed-features` |
   222→| `TABLE_NAME_CATEGORIES_CORRELATIONS` | `categories_correlations` | `category-correlations` |
   223→| `TABLE_NAME_ROOT_CATEGORIES_CORRELATIONS` | `root_cat_correlations` | `root-categories-correlations` |
   224→| `TABLE_NAME_TRENDING_PRODUCTS` | `trending_products` | `forecasted-trending-products` |
   225→| `TABLE_NAME_PRODUCT_ANNUAL_SALES` | `product_annual_sales` | `product-annual-sales` |
   226→| `TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS` | `promotional_products` | `promotional-products` |
   227→| `TABLE_NAME_DEFAULT_UNIT_PRICES` | `default_unit_prices` | `default-unit-prices` |
   228→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLES_MV` | `styles_mv` | `styles-mv` |
   229→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLE_ALTERNATIVES` | `style_alternatives` | `style-alternatives` |
   230→| `TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES` | `style_copurchases` | `style-copurchases` (FBT) |
   231→
   232→The `forecasted-trending-products` module is named after the domain concept but the underlying MySQL table is `trending_products` (constant `TABLE_NAME_TRENDING_PRODUCTS`).
   233→
   234→### Redshift Modules (DEPRECATED)
   235→
   236→> **Do not add new logic to Redshift modules.** The project is migrating to BigQuery as the source of truth. Only modify `redshift/` code when explicitly instructed.
   237→
   238→Redshift modules under `redshift/` use `REDSHIFT_READ_CONNECTION` (PostgreSQL) and reference Redshift-native schema-qualified table names as raw strings — they do not have `TABLE_NAME_*` constants in `db.constant.ts`:
   239→
   240→| Module | Redshift tables used |
   241→|---|---|
   242→| `redshift/orders` | `orders` |
   243→| `redshift/trending-items` | `general_use.customer_orders`, `factdata.order_items`, `factdata.product_information` |
   244→
   245→## Service Layer
   246→
   247→The service is a thin facade that delegates to the model. Apply `@LogExecution` to methods that call third-party services or benefit from performance tracking.
   248→
   249→```typescript
   250→import { Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';
   251→import { LogExecution } from '@decorators/log-execution.decorator';
   252→import { DomainModel } from './models/domain.model';
   253→
   254→@Injectable()
   255→export class DomainService {
   256→  constructor(private readonly domainModel: DomainModel) {}
   257→
   258→  async getById({ id }: { id: string }): Promise<IDomainBase | null> {
   259→    return this.domainModel.findById({ id });
   260→  }
   261→
   262→  @LogExecution({
   263→    paramFormatter: (params) => params,
   264→    responseFormatter: () => '',
   265→  })
   266→  async getByIds({ ids }: { ids: string[] }): Promise<IDomainBase[]> {
   267→    return this.domainModel.findByIds({ ids });
   268→  }
   269→}
   270→```
   271→
   272→## Migrations
   273→
   274→- Create: `npm run migrate:make <name>` — generates a timestamped file in `src/migrations/`.
   275→- Never edit an existing migration file. Add a new migration instead.
   276→- Always wrap schema changes in `knex.transaction`.
   277→- Export both `up` and `down` functions typed as `(knex: Knex) => Promise<void>`.
   278→- Use a `Logger` inside the migration file for progress logs.
   279→- Import table name constants from `@constants/db.constant` rather than using raw strings.
   280→
   281→```typescript
   282→import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
   283→import { TABLE_NAME_X } from '@constants/db.constant';
   284→import type { Knex } from 'knex';
   285→
   286→const logger = new Logger('20260101000000_create_x_table.ts');
   287→
   288→export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
   289→  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
   290→    await trx.schema.createTable(TABLE_NAME_X, (table) => {
   291→      table.uuid('_id').primary();
   292→      table.bigint('style_id').notNullable();
   293→      table.timestamp('created_at').defaultTo(trx.fn.now());
   294→      table.timestamp('updated_at').defaultTo(trx.fn.now());
   295→      table.timestamp('deleted_at').nullable();
   296→      table.index(['style_id', 'deleted_at']);
   297→      table.unique(['style_id'], { predicate: trx.whereNull('deleted_at') });
   298→    });
   299→    logger.log('Created table');
   300→  });
   301→}
   302→
   303→export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
   304→  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
   305→    await trx.schema.dropTableIfExists(TABLE_NAME_X);
   306→  });
   307→}
   308→```
   309→
   310→## Knex Query Builder
   311→
   312→Use the Knex query builder API. Avoid raw SQL unless there is no builder equivalent (e.g., `orderByRaw('COUNT(*) desc')`). Keep raw SQL minimal and contained.
   313→
   314→## Registering New Models
   315→
   316→After creating a new model and service, register both as `providers` and `exports` in `db.module.ts`.
   317→

toolUseResult.prompt:
Explore /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/db/ thoroughly. I need to understand:

1. How Knex.js is configured (read/write replicas, connection pooling, etc.)
2. The db.module.ts setup — how it integrates with NestJS
3. Any base repository patterns, query builders, or abstractions
4. How migrations are handled
5. The CLAUDE.md in that db directory
6. Check package.json for knex version and related packages

Thoroughness: very thorough

Report the full patterns used so we can replicate them.

content.text:
Knex.js with `nest-knexjs`, read/write replicas, same patterns as your recommendations-service. Makes sense — you already know the stack.

Key things I see from that codebase:
- **knex ^3.1.0** + **nest-knexjs ^0.0.34** + **mysql2** (but we'll use **pg** for PostgreSQL)
- Read/write connection separation via named connections
- Soft deletes everywhere, UUID PKs, thin service layer over models
- Migrations with transactions, table name constants

One difference: your existing service uses **MySQL**. We'll adapt the same patterns for **PostgreSQL** (e.g., `pg` client instead of `mysql2`, native UUID type, `timestamp with time zone`).

Next question:

**Session management — how should we handle the authenticated session after OAuth login?**

Options:
- **A) JWT (access + refresh tokens)** — Stateless, stored client-side. Access token short-lived (15min), refresh token longer (7d). Standard for SPAs.
- **B) Server-side sessions** — Session stored in Redis/DB, session ID in httpOnly cookie. Simpler revocation but requires session store.
- **C) JWT with server-side revocation list** — Hybrid: JWT for performance, but track revoked tokens in Redis for logout/security.

My lean: **A** — JWT pair. Fits a React SPA well, no session store needed at this stage, and your current backend is stateless Fastify.

Solid pick. Best of both worlds — stateless auth with the ability to kill sessions when needed.

Next question:

**Where to store the revocation list — Redis or PostgreSQL?**

Options:
- **A) Redis** — Fast, TTL-based auto-expiry (tokens clean themselves up), purpose-built for this. Adds an infrastructure dependency.
- **B) PostgreSQL table** — No new infra, but you need a cleanup job to purge expired tokens. Slower lookups on every request.
- **C) Decide later** — Abstract behind an interface, implement with PG first, swap to Redis when needed.

My lean: **A** if you already plan to use Redis anywhere else in the stack. **C** if you want to keep infra minimal for now.

message.content: sorry i just understood that i'd like to have  B) Server-side sessions active session etc. + jwt ..store in the redis

content.text:
So: **JWT (access + refresh) + server-side sessions in Redis**. On each request, validate the JWT *and* check the session exists in Redis. Logout = delete the session from Redis, invalidating the JWT immediately.

Clear. Moving on.

Next question:

**Role model — what roles do you need beyond company admin?**

Options:
- **A) Just admin for now** — Single role per company, add more later
- **B) Admin + member** — Admins manage company, members have read-only access
- **C) Admin + custom roles** — RBAC with configurable permissions per company

My lean: **A** — YAGNI. You mentioned "later we will add other admins." Start with admin-only, design the schema to support roles later without migration headaches.

Next question:

**Account linking — can one email exist across multiple OAuth providers?**

Example: user signs up with GitHub (john@x.com), later tries Google with same email.

Options:
- **A) Auto-link** — Same email = same account, link both providers automatically
- **B) Separate accounts** — Each provider creates a distinct account, even with same email
- **C) Prompt user** — Detect the conflict, ask "link to existing account?"

My lean: **A** — least friction, and both GitHub/Google verify emails. But has security implications if a provider doesn't verify email.

Next question:

**Can a user belong to multiple companies, or strictly one?**

Options:
- **A) One company only** — Simple. User is bound to one company.
- **B) Multiple companies** — User can switch between companies. Needs a junction table and company context switching.

This matters for your schema design — especially since engineers loaded from Jira/GitHub might overlap across companies.

message.content: 1 admin for 1 company, but developers can be linked for multiples companies

content.text:
Important distinction. So two different entity types:

- **Admin** — user who signs up via OAuth, creates/manages a company. 1:1 with company.
- **Engineer** — data entity loaded from Jira/GitHub. Not a login user. Can belong to multiple companies.

Quick clarification:

**Are engineers ever going to log into the app, or are they purely data records that admins view stats about?**

message.content: yes they can login, but they only will have read rights in the app..they will look only data about themselfs and some other company statisctcs

content.text:
So engineers are also login users (OAuth) but with read-only access scoped to their own data + company stats. Three roles then:

- **Admin** — creates company, manages it. 1:1 with company.
- **Member (engineer)** — invited/linked, can belong to multiple companies. Read-only: own data + company stats.

Next question:

**How do engineers get into the system?**

Options:
- **A) Admin invites by email** — Admin sends invite, engineer signs up via OAuth and gets linked to that company
- **B) Auto-created from Jira/GitHub sync** — When admin connects Jira/GitHub, engineer records are created. Engineers later claim their account by signing up with matching email
- **C) Both** — Invite flow + auto-matching from synced data

Last question before I propose approaches:

**Token lifetimes — any preferences?**

Options:
- **A) Short-lived** — Access token 15min, refresh token 7 days
- **B) Medium** — Access token 1hr, refresh token 30 days
- **C) You decide**

Here are 3 approaches for the auth architecture:

---

### Approach A: Passport.js + NestJS Guards (Classic)

- `@nestjs/passport` with Google/GitHub strategies
- Passport handles OAuth dance, NestJS guards protect routes
- Session stored in Redis via `connect-redis`
- JWT issued after OAuth callback

**Pros:** Battle-tested, huge ecosystem, tons of examples
**Cons:** Passport is a heavy abstraction for just 2 providers. Middleware-oriented design feels awkward in NestJS's decorator-based architecture. Passport v0.7 still has quirks with Fastify.

---

### Approach B: Manual OAuth + Custom Guards (Lean)

- Implement OAuth flows directly (HTTP calls to Google/GitHub token endpoints via `@nestjs/axios` or `fetch`)
- Custom NestJS guards + decorators for auth/roles
- JWT signing via `@nestjs/jwt`
- Redis sessions via `ioredis`

**Pros:** No Passport overhead, full control, cleaner Fastify compatibility, easier to debug. Fewer dependencies.
**Cons:** More code to write upfront. You own the OAuth token exchange logic.

---

### Approach C: Arctic + Custom Guards (Modern)

- [Arctic](https://arcticjs.dev/) — lightweight OAuth 2.0 library, provider-specific helpers for Google/GitHub. Zero dependencies.
- Same custom guards/decorators as B
- Same JWT + Redis session pattern

**Pros:** Cleaner than raw HTTP calls, no Passport bloat, built for modern runtimes. Handles provider quirks (PKCE, token refresh) without you writing it.
**Cons:** Newer library, smaller community than Passport.

---

### My recommendation: **B or C**

Passport is overkill for 2 OAuth providers on Fastify. Between B and C — **C (Arctic)** saves you from writing OAuth token exchange boilerplate while staying lightweight. But if you prefer zero external auth libs, **B** is fine too since the OAuth flow is ~50 lines per provider.

Which direction?

content.text:
Perfect. Now let me create a comprehensive report with all the information gathered:

## Complete Redis Caching Pattern Analysis

Based on my thorough exploration of `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/cache/`, here's the complete pattern for Redis configuration with NestJS, including read/write separation, clustering, and locking mechanisms:

---

### 1. **CACHE MODULE SETUP** (`cache.module.ts`)

The module configures:

- **Two separate Redis connections** (read/write separation):
  - `REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT`: Primary write connection from `REDIS_URL`
  - `REDIS_READ_CLIENT`: Read replica connection from `REDIS_READ_URL`
  
- **Two Keyv cache managers** for abstraction:
  - `CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER`: Uses write URL
  - `CACHE_READ_MANAGER`: Uses read URL

- **Keyv configuration** (via `@keyv/redis`):
  ```
  - namespace: `${SERVER_NAME}:${NODE_ENV}` (e.g., "reco-ms:development")
  - useUnlink: true (faster async deletion vs UNLINK)
  - keyPrefixSeparator: ':'
  ```

- **ioredis options** for write client:
  ```typescript
  retryStrategy: (times) => Math.min(times * 50, 2000)
  maxRetriesPerRequest: 3
  enableReadyCheck: true
  connectTimeout: 10000
  lazyConnect: false
  reconnectOnError: (err) => err.message.includes('READONLY')
  ```

- **ioredis options** for read client:
  ```typescript
  readOnly: true  // Enforces read-only mode
  (same retry strategy as write)
  ```

---

### 2. **CACHE SERVICE** (`cache.service.ts`)

**Core Methods:**

- `get<T>()` - Reads from cache with consistency control:
  - `ConsistencyLevel.EVENTUAL` → reads from replica (if healthy)
  - `ConsistencyLevel.STRONG` → reads from master
  - Falls back to master on replica failure

- `set()` - Writes with TTL support:
  - Tracks write timestamps in `recentWrites` Map
  - Supports optional `ttl` parameter (milliseconds)

- `has()` - Key existence check (via `get()`)

- `deleteOne()` - Single key deletion

- `deleteAll()` - Clear all keys (with 100ms delay between manager calls)

- `incrementByOne()` - Atomic increment via Redis INCR command

**Locking Mechanism (Redlock pattern):**

- `withLock<T>()` - Wrapper for operations with automatic lock release:
  ```typescript
  await cacheService.withLock({
    lockKey: 'my-lock',
    operation: async () => { /* your code */ },
    ttl: 5000  // default 5s
  })
  ```

- `acquireLock()` - Manual lock acquisition
- `releaseLock()` - Manual lock release

**Redlock Configuration:**
```typescript
new Redlock([redisWriteClient], {
  driftFactor: 0.01,
  retryCount: 10,
  retryDelay: 200,
  retryJitter: 200,
})
```

**Smart Read/Write Logic:**

- `shouldReadFromMaster()` checks:
  1. Consistency level requirement
  2. Replica health status
  3. Recent write window (1000ms) - routes to master if key written recently
  4. `recentWrites` Map tracks last write time for each key
  5. Automatic cleanup of entries older than 2 seconds

**Health Checks:**

- Listens to `redisReadClient` 'error' and 'ready' events
- Sets `isReadReplicaHealthy` flag
- Automatic fallback on replica error

---

### 3. **CONSTANTS & ENV VARIABLES**

**Environment Variables** (`env-variables.constant.ts`):
```typescript
REDIS_URL          // Primary write connection
REDIS_READ_URL     // Read replica connection
NODE_ENV            // For namespacing
SERVER_NAME         // For namespacing
```

**Provider Constants** (`common.constant.ts`):
```typescript
REDIS_READ_CLIENT   // 'REDIS_READ_CLIENT'
REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT  // 'REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT'
CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER // 'CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER'
CACHE_READ_MANAGER  // 'CACHE_READ_MANAGER'
```

**Cache Namespace Enum** (`cache.interface.ts`):
```typescript
enum Namespaces {
  RELATED_ITEMS_MODEL = 'related-items-model',
  FBT_MODEL = 'fbt-model',
  MMS_COMPLEMENTS = 'mms-complements',
  MMS_ALTERNATIVES = 'mms-alternatives',
  HEALTH_CHECK = 'health-check',
  METRICS = 'metrics',
  CURATED_ADDONS = 'curated-addons',
}
```

**.env.example** entries:
```
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6377
REDIS_READ_URL=redis://localhost:6378
RELATED_PRODUCTS_CACHE_TTL=129600000  # 36 hours
CURATED_ADDONS_CACHE_TTL=18000000    # 5 hours
```

---

### 4. **NPM PACKAGES** (`package.json`)

**Required Dependencies:**
```json
"ioredis": "^5.10.0"              // Redis client
"@keyv/redis": "^5.1.6"           // Keyv Redis adapter
"@nestjs/cache-manager": "^3.1.0" // NestJS cache abstraction
"redlock": "^5.0.0-beta.2"        // Distributed locking
```

---

### 5. **ERROR HANDLING**

**Custom CacheError interface:**
```typescript
interface CacheError extends Error {
  code: 'CONNECTION_FAILED' | 'KEY_NOT_FOUND' | 'OPERATION_FAILED'
  originalError?: Error
}
```

**Error scenarios:**
- Connection failures → throw `CONNECTION_FAILED`
- Operation failures → throw `OPERATION_FAILED`
- Replica fails → log warning, fallback to master with `STRONG` consistency
- Master fails → propagate error

---

### 6. **USAGE PATTERN** (from metrics.service.ts)

```typescript
@Injectable()
export class MetricsService {
  constructor(private readonly cacheService: CacheService) {}

  async incrementDailyCounter({ metricType }: { metricType: string }) {
    const today = new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0];
    const key = `${metricType}:${today}`;
    await this.cacheService.incrementByOne({ 
      namespace: Namespaces.METRICS, 
      key 
    });
  }
}
```

---

### 7. **KEY ARCHITECTURAL DECISIONS**

1. **Dual Keyv managers** - Allows different Redis URLs for read/write without changing client code
2. **Recent writes tracking** - Prevents stale reads within 1-second window after writes
3. **Eventual vs Strong consistency** - Trade-off control at call site
4. **Redlock on write client only** - Locks only need to block writes, not reads
5. **Health checks on read replica** - Graceful degradation when replica fails
6. **Async-friendly unlink** - `useUnlink: true` prevents blocking during deletes
7. **Namespacing** - Isolates different domains and environments automatically

---

Perfect. Now let me create a comprehensive report with all the information gathered:

## Complete Redis Caching Pattern Analysis

Based on my thorough exploration of `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/cache/`, here's the complete pattern for Redis configuration with NestJS, including read/write separation, clustering, and locking mechanisms:

---

### 1. **CACHE MODULE SETUP** (`cache.module.ts`)

The module configures:

- **Two separate Redis connections** (read/write separation):
  - `REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT`: Primary write connection from `REDIS_URL`
  - `REDIS_READ_CLIENT`: Read replica connection from `REDIS_READ_URL`
  
- **Two Keyv cache managers** for abstraction:
  - `CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER`: Uses write URL
  - `CACHE_READ_MANAGER`: Uses read URL

- **Keyv configuration** (via `@keyv/redis`):
  ```
  - namespace: `${SERVER_NAME}:${NODE_ENV}` (e.g., "reco-ms:development")
  - useUnlink: true (faster async deletion vs UNLINK)
  - keyPrefixSeparator: ':'
  ```

- **ioredis options** for write client:
  ```typescript
  retryStrategy: (times) => Math.min(times * 50, 2000)
  maxRetriesPerRequest: 3
  enableReadyCheck: true
  connectTimeout: 10000
  lazyConnect: false
  reconnectOnError: (err) => err.message.includes('READONLY')
  ```

- **ioredis options** for read client:
  ```typescript
  readOnly: true  // Enforces read-only mode
  (same retry strategy as write)
  ```

---

### 2. **CACHE SERVICE** (`cache.service.ts`)

**Core Methods:**

- `get<T>()` - Reads from cache with consistency control:
  - `ConsistencyLevel.EVENTUAL` → reads from replica (if healthy)
  - `ConsistencyLevel.STRONG` → reads from master
  - Falls back to master on replica failure

- `set()` - Writes with TTL support:
  - Tracks write timestamps in `recentWrites` Map
  - Supports optional `ttl` parameter (milliseconds)

- `has()` - Key existence check (via `get()`)

- `deleteOne()` - Single key deletion

- `deleteAll()` - Clear all keys (with 100ms delay between manager calls)

- `incrementByOne()` - Atomic increment via Redis INCR command

**Locking Mechanism (Redlock pattern):**

- `withLock<T>()` - Wrapper for operations with automatic lock release:
  ```typescript
  await cacheService.withLock({
    lockKey: 'my-lock',
    operation: async () => { /* your code */ },
    ttl: 5000  // default 5s
  })
  ```

- `acquireLock()` - Manual lock acquisition
- `releaseLock()` - Manual lock release

**Redlock Configuration:**
```typescript
new Redlock([redisWriteClient], {
  driftFactor: 0.01,
  retryCount: 10,
  retryDelay: 200,
  retryJitter: 200,
})
```

**Smart Read/Write Logic:**

- `shouldReadFromMaster()` checks:
  1. Consistency level requirement
  2. Replica health status
  3. Recent write window (1000ms) - routes to master if key written recently
  4. `recentWrites` Map tracks last write time for each key
  5. Automatic cleanup of entries older than 2 seconds

**Health Checks:**

- Listens to `redisReadClient` 'error' and 'ready' events
- Sets `isReadReplicaHealthy` flag
- Automatic fallback on replica error

---

### 3. **CONSTANTS & ENV VARIABLES**

**Environment Variables** (`env-variables.constant.ts`):
```typescript
REDIS_URL          // Primary write connection
REDIS_READ_URL     // Read replica connection
NODE_ENV            // For namespacing
SERVER_NAME         // For namespacing
```

**Provider Constants** (`common.constant.ts`):
```typescript
REDIS_READ_CLIENT   // 'REDIS_READ_CLIENT'
REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT  // 'REDIS_WRITE_CLIENT'
CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER // 'CACHE_WRITE_MANAGER'
CACHE_READ_MANAGER  // 'CACHE_READ_MANAGER'
```

**Cache Namespace Enum** (`cache.interface.ts`):
```typescript
enum Namespaces {
  RELATED_ITEMS_MODEL = 'related-items-model',
  FBT_MODEL = 'fbt-model',
  MMS_COMPLEMENTS = 'mms-complements',
  MMS_ALTERNATIVES = 'mms-alternatives',
  HEALTH_CHECK = 'health-check',
  METRICS = 'metrics',
  CURATED_ADDONS = 'curated-addons',
}
```

**.env.example** entries:
```
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6377
REDIS_READ_URL=redis://localhost:6378
RELATED_PRODUCTS_CACHE_TTL=129600000  # 36 hours
CURATED_ADDONS_CACHE_TTL=18000000    # 5 hours
```

---

### 4. **NPM PACKAGES** (`package.json`)

**Required Dependencies:**
```json
"ioredis": "^5.10.0"              // Redis client
"@keyv/redis": "^5.1.6"           // Keyv Redis adapter
"@nestjs/cache-manager": "^3.1.0" // NestJS cache abstraction
"redlock": "^5.0.0-beta.2"        // Distributed locking
```

---

### 5. **ERROR HANDLING**

**Custom CacheError interface:**
```typescript
interface CacheError extends Error {
  code: 'CONNECTION_FAILED' | 'KEY_NOT_FOUND' | 'OPERATION_FAILED'
  originalError?: Error
}
```

**Error scenarios:**
- Connection failures → throw `CONNECTION_FAILED`
- Operation failures → throw `OPERATION_FAILED`
- Replica fails → log warning, fallback to master with `STRONG` consistency
- Master fails → propagate error

---

### 6. **USAGE PATTERN** (from metrics.service.ts)

```typescript
@Injectable()
export class MetricsService {
  constructor(private readonly cacheService: CacheService) {}

  async incrementDailyCounter({ metricType }: { metricType: string }) {
    const today = new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0];
    const key = `${metricType}:${today}`;
    await this.cacheService.incrementByOne({ 
      namespace: Namespaces.METRICS, 
      key 
    });
  }
}
```

---

### 7. **KEY ARCHITECTURAL DECISIONS**

1. **Dual Keyv managers** - Allows different Redis URLs for read/write without changing client code
2. **Recent writes tracking** - Prevents stale reads within 1-second window after writes
3. **Eventual vs Strong consistency** - Trade-off control at call site
4. **Redlock on write client only** - Locks only need to block writes, not reads
5. **Health checks on read replica** - Graceful degradation when replica fails
6. **Async-friendly unlink** - `useUnlink: true` prevents blocking during deletes
7. **Namespacing** - Isolates different domains and environments automatically

---

### **File Locations**

- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/cache/cache.module.ts`
- `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/cache/cache.service.ts`

Got it. Your Redis setup uses `ioredis` with read/write separation, `@keyv/redis` for caching, `redlock` for distributed locks, and health-based fallback. We'll replicate this pattern for session storage.

Let me present the design section by section. Tell me after each if it looks right.

---

## Section 1: Data Model

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ users                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ _id          UUID PK                     │
│ email        VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL │
│ name         VARCHAR(255)                │
│ avatar_url   VARCHAR(512)                │
│ role         ENUM('admin','member')      │
│ company_id   UUID FK → companies._id    │  ← NULL for members (use junction)
│ created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ updated_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ deleted_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ oauth_accounts                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ _id          UUID PK                     │
│ user_id      UUID FK → users._id        │
│ provider     ENUM('github','google')     │
│ provider_id  VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL       │
│ email        VARCHAR(255)                │
│ access_token TEXT (encrypted)            │
│ created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ updated_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ deleted_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│                                          │
│ UNIQUE(provider, provider_id)            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ companies                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ _id          UUID PK                     │
│ name         VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL       │
│ created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ updated_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ deleted_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ company_members                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ _id          UUID PK                     │
│ user_id      UUID FK → users._id        │
│ company_id   UUID FK → companies._id    │
│ role         ENUM('admin','member')      │
│ created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ updated_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ deleted_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│                                          │
│ UNIQUE(user_id, company_id)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ invitations                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ _id          UUID PK                     │
│ company_id   UUID FK → companies._id    │
│ email        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL       │
│ role         ENUM('admin','member')      │
│ token        VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE         │
│ expires_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ accepted_at  TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ updated_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
│ deleted_at   TIMESTAMPTZ                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key decisions:**
- `company_members` is the junction table — members can belong to multiple companies, admins are 1:1 (enforced in app logic)
- `oauth_accounts` separate from `users` — supports multiple providers per user + the "prompt to link" flow
- `invitations` table tracks pending invites with expiry tokens
- `users.company_id` is the admin's "owned" company — quick lookup without joining. NULL for pure members.
- All tables follow your existing patterns: UUID PK, soft deletes, timestamps

Does this look right?

message.content: oauth_accounts and users should be separate or can be 1 table? explain

content.text:
Two valid approaches:

**Separate tables (current proposal):**
- One user can have multiple OAuth accounts (GitHub + Google)
- Clean normalization — user data lives in `users`, provider data in `oauth_accounts`
- The "prompt to link" flow you chose requires looking up existing accounts by provider — separate table makes this a simple query
- Adding a third provider later = just insert a row, no schema change

**Merged into one table:**
- Simpler queries for login (one table hit)
- But: if a user links both GitHub and Google, you need either duplicate user rows or multiple `provider_*` column pairs (`github_id`, `google_id`, `github_token`, `google_token`...)
- Adding a new provider = schema migration to add columns
- Conflict detection ("this email exists with a different provider") becomes messier

**Verdict:** Separate tables. Your requirements demand it — multiple providers per user + account linking prompt. Merging would fight the data model.

Stay with the current design?

message.content: stay with current design..1 question..why we have access tocket in the db if our plan is to store sessions in the redis?

content.text:
Different tokens:

- **`oauth_accounts.access_token`** — the token from GitHub/Google API. You need this to **call their APIs** (fetch repos, Jira data, user profile updates). Without storing it, you'd have to re-authenticate every time you want to pull data from GitHub/Jira.

- **Session in Redis** — your app's session. Tracks "this user is logged in." Has nothing to do with GitHub/Google APIs.

They serve different purposes. The OAuth access token is a credential for external APIs, not for your app's auth.

**However** — if you're only using GitHub/Google for *login identity* and won't call their APIs from your backend, then you don't need to store it. You'd only need `provider` + `provider_id` to identify the user.

**Will your backend call GitHub/Google APIs on behalf of users?**
