content.text:
Good. Moving on.

## Section 2: Auth Flow

```
┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌───────┐
│  React   │     │ Backend  │     │ GitHub/   │     │ Redis │
│  SPA     │     │ NestJS   │     │ Google    │     │       │
└────┬─────┘     └────┬─────┘     └─────┬─────┘     └───┬───┘
     │                │                  │               │
     │ GET /auth/github                  │               │
     │───────────────>│                  │               │
     │                │  redirect to provider            │
     │<───────────────│                  │               │
     │                │                  │               │
     │ ──── browser redirect ──────────>│               │
     │                │                  │               │
     │ callback with code               │               │
     │───────────────>│                  │               │
     │                │  exchange code   │               │
     │                │────────────────>│               │
     │                │  access_token   │               │
     │                │<────────────────│               │
     │                │  fetch profile  │               │
     │                │────────────────>│               │
     │                │  user data      │               │
     │                │<────────────────│               │
     │                │                  │               │
     │                │  ┌─────────────────────────┐    │
     │                │  │ Lookup oauth_accounts   │    │
     │                │  │ by provider+provider_id │    │
     │                │  │                         │    │
     │                │  │ EXISTS + SAME USER?     │    │
     │                │  │  → login                │    │
     │                │  │                         │    │
     │                │  │ EXISTS + DIFF USER?     │    │
     │                │  │  → prompt to link       │    │
     │                │  │                         │    │
     │                │  │ NOT EXISTS + email match?│    │
     │                │  │  → prompt to link       │    │
     │                │  │                         │    │
     │                │  │ NOT EXISTS + no match?  │    │
     │                │  │  → new signup flow      │    │
     │                │  └─────────────────────────┘    │
     │                │                  │               │
     │                │  create session  │               │
     │                │──────────────────────────────>│
     │                │                  │               │
     │  JWT (access + refresh)           │               │
     │<───────────────│                  │               │
     │                │                  │               │
```

**On every authenticated request:**
1. Validate JWT signature + expiry
2. Check session exists in Redis (key: `session:{user_id}:{session_id}`)
3. If session gone → 401, force re-login

**Token refresh:**
1. Client sends refresh token
2. Backend validates it, checks session in Redis
3. Issues new access token (1hr), rotates refresh token (30d)

**Logout:**
- Delete session from Redis → JWT becomes invalid immediately

Does this flow look right?

## Section 3: Module Architecture

```
backend/src/modules/
├── auth/
│   ├── auth.module.ts
│   ├── auth.controller.ts        # OAuth endpoints, login, logout, refresh
│   ├── auth.service.ts           # OAuth flow orchestration, token issuance
│   ├── strategies/
│   │   ├── github.strategy.ts    # Arctic GitHub provider
│   │   └── google.strategy.ts    # Arctic Google provider
│   ├── guards/
│   │   ├── auth.guard.ts         # JWT + session validation
│   │   └── roles.guard.ts        # Role-based access (admin/member)
│   ├── decorators/
│   │   ├── current-user.decorator.ts   # Extract user from request
│   │   ├── roles.decorator.ts          # @Roles('admin')
│   │   └── public.decorator.ts         # Skip auth for specific routes
│   ├── dto/
│   ├── types/
│   └── constants/
│
├── session/
│   ├── session.module.ts
│   ├── session.service.ts        # Redis session CRUD, uses your cache pattern
│   └── types/
│
├── user/
│   ├── user.module.ts
│   ├── user.service.ts
│   ├── user.model.ts             # Knex queries for users table
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
│
├── oauth-account/
│   ├── oauth-account.module.ts
│   ├── oauth-account.service.ts
│   ├── oauth-account.model.ts    # Knex queries for oauth_accounts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
│
├── company/
│   ├── company.module.ts
│   ├── company.service.ts
│   ├── company.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
│
├── invitation/
│   ├── invitation.module.ts
│   ├── invitation.service.ts
│   ├── invitation.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
│
├── cache/                         # Redis module (replicated from recommendations-service)
│   ├── cache.module.ts
│   └── cache.service.ts
│
└── db/
    └── db.module.ts               # Knex read/write connections (pg client)
```

**Key points:**
- `session` module is separate from `auth` — session is infrastructure, auth is business logic
- `cache` module replicates your existing ioredis pattern with read/write separation + redlock
- Models follow your Knex pattern: inject `KNEX_READ_CONNECTION` / `KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION`, soft deletes, UUID PKs, DTO validation
- Guards are in `auth/guards/` not in the global `guards/` dir — they're auth-specific

Does this structure look right?

## Section 4: API Endpoints

```
# OAuth
GET  /auth/github              → Redirect to GitHub OAuth
GET  /auth/github/callback     → Handle GitHub callback
GET  /auth/google              → Redirect to Google OAuth
GET  /auth/google/callback     → Handle Google callback

# Session
POST /auth/refresh             → Refresh access token
POST /auth/logout              → Destroy session

# Account linking (when conflict detected)
POST /auth/link                → Link OAuth account to existing user

# Signup (new company creation)
POST /auth/signup/company      → Create company after first OAuth login

# User
GET  /users/me                 → Current user profile + active company

# Company
GET  /companies/:id            → Company details (admin: full, member: limited)

# Invitations (admin only)
POST /companies/:id/invitations         → Invite engineer by email
GET  /companies/:id/invitations         → List pending invitations
DELETE /companies/:id/invitations/:id   → Revoke invitation

# Invitation acceptance (public, token-based)
GET  /invitations/accept?token=xxx      → Validate invite, redirect to OAuth
```

**Auth flow detail:**
- OAuth callback returns a **temporary code** (not JWT) to the frontend
- Frontend exchanges code for JWT via a POST — avoids tokens in URL/browser history
- On conflict (email exists with different provider), callback returns a linking prompt token instead

Does this look right?

## Section 5: Session Structure in Redis

```
Key:    session:{user_id}:{session_id}
TTL:    30 days (matches refresh token)

Value:  {
  user_id:      "uuid",
  session_id:   "uuid",
  role:         "admin" | "member",
  company_id:   "uuid" | null,      // active company context
  provider:     "github" | "google",
  ip:           "x.x.x.x",
  user_agent:   "...",
  created_at:   "ISO timestamp",
  last_active:  "ISO timestamp"
}
```

**Session operations:**
- **Create** — on login, write to Redis with 30d TTL
- **Validate** — on every request, auth guard checks key exists
- **Refresh** — on token refresh, update `last_active`
- **Destroy** — on logout, delete key
- **Destroy all** — admin can kill all sessions for a user (password compromise, etc.)

**Pattern for "destroy all":**
- Scan keys matching `session:{user_id}:*` and delete
- Alternatively, maintain a set `sessions:{user_id}` listing all session IDs for O(1) lookup

**Company context switching (for members in multiple companies):**
- Member calls `POST /users/me/company` with `company_id`
- Updates `company_id` in the Redis session
- Next access token issued includes the new company context

Does this look right?

## Section 6: Security Considerations

**OAuth:**
- CSRF protection via `state` parameter in OAuth flow (Arctic handles this)
- PKCE for Google (Arctic supports it natively)
- OAuth tokens encrypted at rest in `oauth_accounts.access_token` via AES-256

**JWT:**
- Signed with RS256 (asymmetric) or HS256 (symmetric). Recommend **HS256** — simpler, single backend, no key distribution needed.
- Access token payload: `{ user_id, session_id, role, company_id }` — minimal claims
- Refresh token: opaque UUID stored alongside session in Redis, not a JWT

**Transport:**
- Cookies: `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite: strict` for refresh token
- Access token: returned in response body, stored in memory (not localStorage) on frontend
- CORS: restrict to your frontend domain

**Rate limiting:**
- `/auth/*` endpoints: strict rate limit (e.g., 10 req/min per IP) via `@nestjs/throttler`
- Token refresh: 5 req/min per user

**Invitation tokens:**
- Cryptographically random (48 bytes, base64url)
- 72hr expiry
- Single use — mark `accepted_at` on use

That's the full design. Does it look right, or anything you'd change?

# Authentication & Authorization Design

## Overview

OAuth-based authentication (GitHub + Google) for a multi-tenant B2B application. Companies are the primary tenant. Admins create and manage companies; members (engineers) are invited and have read-only access to their own data and company statistics.

## Stack

- **OAuth**: Arctic (lightweight OAuth 2.0 library)
- **JWT**: `@nestjs/jwt` — access token (1hr) + refresh token (30d, opaque UUID)
- **Sessions**: Redis (ioredis with read/write separation, replicating recommendations-service cache pattern)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with read/write replicas via Knex.js (`nest-knexjs`)
- **Locking**: Redlock via Redis write client

## Data Model

### users

| Column     | Type              | Constraints               |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| _id        | UUID              | PK                        |
| email      | VARCHAR(255)      | UNIQUE, NOT NULL          |
| name       | VARCHAR(255)      |                           |
| avatar_url | VARCHAR(512)      |                           |
| role       | ENUM(admin,member)| NOT NULL                  |
| company_id | UUID              | FK → companies._id, NULL  |
| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()   |
| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()   |
| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NULL                      |

- `company_id` is the admin's owned company. NULL for pure members.

### oauth_accounts

| Column       | Type                  | Constraints                     |
|--------------|-----------------------|---------------------------------|
| _id          | UUID                  | PK                              |
| user_id      | UUID                  | FK → users._id, NOT NULL        |
| provider     | ENUM(github,google)   | NOT NULL                        |
| provider_id  | VARCHAR(255)          | NOT NULL                        |
| email        | VARCHAR(255)          |                                 |
| access_token | TEXT                  | Encrypted (AES-256)             |
| created_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()         |
| updated_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()         |
| deleted_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NULL                            |

- UNIQUE(provider, provider_id)
- Supports multiple providers per user (account linking)

### companies

| Column     | Type         | Constraints             |
|------------|--------------|-------------------------|
| _id        | UUID         | PK                      |
| name       | VARCHAR(255) | NOT NULL                |
| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now() |
| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now() |
| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NULL                    |

### company_members

| Column     | Type                  | Constraints                  |
|------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
| _id        | UUID                  | PK                           |
| user_id    | UUID                  | FK → users._id, NOT NULL     |
| company_id | UUID                  | FK → companies._id, NOT NULL |
| role       | ENUM(admin,member)    | NOT NULL                     |
| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NULL                         |

- UNIQUE(user_id, company_id)
- Admins: 1:1 with company (enforced in app logic)
- Members: can belong to multiple companies

### invitations

| Column      | Type                | Constraints                  |
|-------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| _id         | UUID                | PK                           |
| company_id  | UUID                | FK → companies._id, NOT NULL |
| email       | VARCHAR(255)        | NOT NULL                     |
| role        | ENUM(admin,member)  | NOT NULL                     |
| token       | VARCHAR(255)        | UNIQUE, NOT NULL             |
| expires_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL                     |
| accepted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NULL                         |
| created_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
| updated_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
| deleted_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NULL                         |

## Auth Flow

### OAuth Login Sequence

1. Frontend redirects to `GET /auth/{provider}`
2. Backend generates state param, redirects to provider
3. Provider redirects back to `GET /auth/{provider}/callback` with code
4. Backend exchanges code for access token via Arctic
5. Backend fetches user profile from provider
6. Account resolution:
   - **Existing oauth_account, same user** → login
   - **Existing oauth_account, different user** → prompt to link
   - **No oauth_account, email matches existing user** → prompt to link
   - **No oauth_account, no email match** → new signup flow
7. Create session in Redis
8. Return JWT (access + refresh) to frontend

### Token Exchange Security

- OAuth callback returns a temporary authorization code to frontend (not JWT)
- Frontend exchanges code for JWT via POST — avoids tokens in URL/browser history

### Token Refresh

1. Client sends refresh token (from httpOnly cookie)
2. Backend validates refresh token, checks session exists in Redis
3. Issues new access token (1hr), rotates refresh token (30d)

### Request Authentication

On every authenticated request:
1. Validate JWT signature + expiry
2. Check session exists in Redis (key: `session:{user_id}:{session_id}`)
3. If session gone → 401, force re-login

### Logout

- Delete session key from Redis → JWT becomes invalid immediately

## Session Structure (Redis)

```
Key:    session:{user_id}:{session_id}
TTL:    30 days

Value:  {
  user_id:      "uuid",
  session_id:   "uuid",
  role:         "admin" | "member",
  company_id:   "uuid" | null,
  provider:     "github" | "google",
  ip:           "x.x.x.x",
  user_agent:   "...",
  created_at:   "ISO timestamp",
  last_active:  "ISO timestamp"
}
```

### Session Operations

- **Create** — on login, write to Redis with 30d TTL
- **Validate** — on every request, auth guard checks key exists
- **Refresh** — on token refresh, update `last_active`
- **Destroy** — on logout, delete key
- **Destroy all** — scan keys `session:{user_id}:*` and delete (or maintain a set `sessions:{user_id}` for O(1) lookup)

### Company Context Switching (Members)

- Member calls `POST /users/me/company` with `company_id`
- Updates `company_id` in Redis session
- Next access token includes new company context

## API Endpoints

### OAuth

| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| GET    | /auth/github               | Redirect to GitHub OAuth       | Public   |
| GET    | /auth/github/callback      | Handle GitHub callback         | Public   |
| GET    | /auth/google               | Redirect to Google OAuth       | Public   |
| GET    | /auth/google/callback      | Handle Google callback         | Public   |
| POST   | /auth/refresh              | Refresh access token           | Cookie   |
| POST   | /auth/logout               | Destroy session                | Auth     |
| POST   | /auth/link                 | Link OAuth to existing user    | Auth     |

### Signup

| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| POST   | /auth/signup/company       | Create company after OAuth     | Auth     |

### User

| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| GET    | /users/me                  | Current user + active company  | Auth     |
| POST   | /users/me/company          | Switch active company context  | Auth     |

### Company

| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| GET    | /companies/:id             | Company details                | Auth     |

### Invitations (Admin only)

| Method | Path                              | Description              | Auth     |
|--------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| POST   | /companies/:id/invitations        | Invite engineer by email | Admin    |
| GET    | /companies/:id/invitations        | List pending invitations | Admin    |
| DELETE | /companies/:id/invitations/:invId | Revoke invitation        | Admin    |
| GET    | /invitations/accept?token=xxx     | Accept invite → OAuth    | Public   |

## Module Architecture

```
backend/src/modules/
├── auth/
│   ├── auth.module.ts
│   ├── auth.controller.ts
│   ├── auth.service.ts
│   ├── strategies/
│   │   ├── github.strategy.ts      # Arctic GitHub provider
│   │   └── google.strategy.ts      # Arctic Google provider
│   ├── guards/
│   │   ├── auth.guard.ts           # JWT + session validation
│   │   └── roles.guard.ts          # Role-based access
│   ├── decorators/
│   │   ├── current-user.decorator.ts
│   │   ├── roles.decorator.ts
│   │   └── public.decorator.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   ├── types/
│   └── constants/
├── session/
│   ├── session.module.ts
│   ├── session.service.ts
│   └── types/
├── user/
│   ├── user.module.ts
│   ├── user.service.ts
│   ├── user.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
├── oauth-account/
│   ├── oauth-account.module.ts
│   ├── oauth-account.service.ts
│   ├── oauth-account.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
├── company/
│   ├── company.module.ts
│   ├── company.service.ts
│   ├── company.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
├── invitation/
│   ├── invitation.module.ts
│   ├── invitation.service.ts
│   ├── invitation.model.ts
│   ├── dto/
│   └── types/
├── cache/
│   ├── cache.module.ts              # ioredis read/write + redlock
│   └── cache.service.ts
└── db/
    └── db.module.ts                 # Knex pg read/write connections
```

## Security

### OAuth

- CSRF protection via `state` parameter (Arctic handles this)
- PKCE for Google (Arctic native support)
- OAuth access tokens encrypted at rest (AES-256) in `oauth_accounts.access_token`

### JWT

- Signed with HS256 (symmetric, single backend)
- Access token payload: `{ user_id, session_id, role, company_id }` — minimal claims
- Refresh token: opaque UUID stored alongside session in Redis (not a JWT)

### Transport

- Refresh token: `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite: strict` cookie
- Access token: response body, stored in memory on frontend (not localStorage)
- CORS: restricted to frontend domain

### Rate Limiting

- `/auth/*` endpoints: 10 req/min per IP (`@nestjs/throttler`)
- Token refresh: 5 req/min per user

### Invitation Tokens

- Cryptographically random (48 bytes, base64url)
- 72hr expiry
- Single use — `accepted_at` set on use

## Infrastructure Dependencies

### PostgreSQL

- Write primary + read replica(s)
- Knex.js via `nest-knexjs` with named connections (`KNEX_READ_CONNECTION`, `KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION`)
- Client: `pg`

### Redis

- Write primary + read replica
- ioredis with read/write separation (replicating recommendations-service pattern)
- Keyv for cache abstraction
- Redlock for distributed locking
- Session TTL: 30 days

### NPM Packages (New)

- `arctic` — OAuth 2.0 provider helpers
- `@nestjs/jwt` — JWT signing/verification
- `pg` — PostgreSQL client
- `knex` + `nest-knexjs` — query builder + NestJS integration
- `ioredis` — Redis client
- `@keyv/redis` — Keyv Redis adapter
- `redlock` — distributed locking
- `@nestjs/throttler` — rate limiting

## Patterns (Following recommendations-service)

### Database Layer

- Knex read/write connection separation
- Soft deletes (`.whereNull('deleted_at')` on all reads)
- UUID primary keys via `randomUUID()` from `node:crypto`
- Timestamps via `knex.fn.now()`
- Input/output DTO validation via `plainToInstance()` + `validate()`
- Thin service layer delegating to models
- Table name constants (never raw strings)
- Migrations wrapped in transactions, idempotent

### Redis/Cache Layer

- ioredis read/write clients with health-based fallback
- Keyv cache managers with namespace isolation
- Redlock for distributed locking
- Recent-writes tracking for read-after-write consistency

input.prompt:
Review the design spec at /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-22-auth-design.md

This is an authentication and authorization design for a multi-tenant B2B NestJS app. Check for:

1. **Completeness** — are there gaps in the auth flows, edge cases not covered?
2. **Consistency** — do the data model, API endpoints, and session design align?
3. **Security** — any vulnerabilities or missing security measures?
4. **Feasibility** — any design decisions that would be hard to implement or maintain?
5. **Contradictions** — anything that conflicts with itself?

Context:
- NestJS + Fastify backend, React frontend
- OAuth via Arctic (GitHub + Google)
- JWT access (1hr) + opaque refresh token (30d)
- Sessions in Redis with read/write separation
- PostgreSQL with read/write replicas via Knex.js
- Multi-tenant: companies with admin (1:1) and member (many:many) roles
- Follows patterns from an existing recommendations-service (Knex, ioredis, redlock)

Report issues categorized as Critical / Major / Minor / Suggestions.

content.content:
1→# Authentication & Authorization Design
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→OAuth-based authentication (GitHub + Google) for a multi-tenant B2B application. Companies are the primary tenant. Admins create and manage companies; members (engineers) are invited and have read-only access to their own data and company statistics.
     6→
     7→## Stack
     8→
     9→- **OAuth**: Arctic (lightweight OAuth 2.0 library)
    10→- **JWT**: `@nestjs/jwt` — access token (1hr) + refresh token (30d, opaque UUID)
    11→- **Sessions**: Redis (ioredis with read/write separation, replicating recommendations-service cache pattern)
    12→- **Database**: PostgreSQL with read/write replicas via Knex.js (`nest-knexjs`)
    13→- **Locking**: Redlock via Redis write client
    14→
    15→## Data Model
    16→
    17→### users
    18→
    19→| Column     | Type              | Constraints               |
    20→|------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
    21→| _id        | UUID              | PK                        |
    22→| email      | VARCHAR(255)      | UNIQUE, NOT NULL          |
    23→| name       | VARCHAR(255)      |                           |
    24→| avatar_url | VARCHAR(512)      |                           |
    25→| role       | ENUM(admin,member)| NOT NULL                  |
    26→| company_id | UUID              | FK → companies._id, NULL  |
    27→| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()   |
    28→| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()   |
    29→| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ       | NULL                      |
    30→
    31→- `company_id` is the admin's owned company. NULL for pure members.
    32→
    33→### oauth_accounts
    34→
    35→| Column       | Type                  | Constraints                     |
    36→|--------------|-----------------------|---------------------------------|
    37→| _id          | UUID                  | PK                              |
    38→| user_id      | UUID                  | FK → users._id, NOT NULL        |
    39→| provider     | ENUM(github,google)   | NOT NULL                        |
    40→| provider_id  | VARCHAR(255)          | NOT NULL                        |
    41→| email        | VARCHAR(255)          |                                 |
    42→| access_token | TEXT                  | Encrypted (AES-256)             |
    43→| created_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()         |
    44→| updated_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()         |
    45→| deleted_at   | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NULL                            |
    46→
    47→- UNIQUE(provider, provider_id)
    48→- Supports multiple providers per user (account linking)
    49→
    50→### companies
    51→
    52→| Column     | Type         | Constraints             |
    53→|------------|--------------|-------------------------|
    54→| _id        | UUID         | PK                      |
    55→| name       | VARCHAR(255) | NOT NULL                |
    56→| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now() |
    57→| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now() |
    58→| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ  | NULL                    |
    59→
    60→### company_members
    61→
    62→| Column     | Type                  | Constraints                  |
    63→|------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
    64→| _id        | UUID                  | PK                           |
    65→| user_id    | UUID                  | FK → users._id, NOT NULL     |
    66→| company_id | UUID                  | FK → companies._id, NOT NULL |
    67→| role       | ENUM(admin,member)    | NOT NULL                     |
    68→| created_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
    69→| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
    70→| deleted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ           | NULL                         |
    71→
    72→- UNIQUE(user_id, company_id)
    73→- Admins: 1:1 with company (enforced in app logic)
    74→- Members: can belong to multiple companies
    75→
    76→### invitations
    77→
    78→| Column      | Type                | Constraints                  |
    79→|-------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
    80→| _id         | UUID                | PK                           |
    81→| company_id  | UUID                | FK → companies._id, NOT NULL |
    82→| email       | VARCHAR(255)        | NOT NULL                     |
    83→| role        | ENUM(admin,member)  | NOT NULL                     |
    84→| token       | VARCHAR(255)        | UNIQUE, NOT NULL             |
    85→| expires_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL                     |
    86→| accepted_at | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NULL                         |
    87→| created_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
    88→| updated_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NOT NULL, DEFAULT now()      |
    89→| deleted_at  | TIMESTAMPTZ         | NULL                         |
    90→
    91→## Auth Flow
    92→
    93→### OAuth Login Sequence
    94→
    95→1. Frontend redirects to `GET /auth/{provider}`
    96→2. Backend generates state param, redirects to provider
    97→3. Provider redirects back to `GET /auth/{provider}/callback` with code
    98→4. Backend exchanges code for access token via Arctic
    99→5. Backend fetches user profile from provider
   100→6. Account resolution:
   101→   - **Existing oauth_account, same user** → login
   102→   - **Existing oauth_account, different user** → prompt to link
   103→   - **No oauth_account, email matches existing user** → prompt to link
   104→   - **No oauth_account, no email match** → new signup flow
   105→7. Create session in Redis
   106→8. Return JWT (access + refresh) to frontend
   107→
   108→### Token Exchange Security
   109→
   110→- OAuth callback returns a temporary authorization code to frontend (not JWT)
   111→- Frontend exchanges code for JWT via POST — avoids tokens in URL/browser history
   112→
   113→### Token Refresh
   114→
   115→1. Client sends refresh token (from httpOnly cookie)
   116→2. Backend validates refresh token, checks session exists in Redis
   117→3. Issues new access token (1hr), rotates refresh token (30d)
   118→
   119→### Request Authentication
   120→
   121→On every authenticated request:
   122→1. Validate JWT signature + expiry
   123→2. Check session exists in Redis (key: `session:{user_id}:{session_id}`)
   124→3. If session gone → 401, force re-login
   125→
   126→### Logout
   127→
   128→- Delete session key from Redis → JWT becomes invalid immediately
   129→
   130→## Session Structure (Redis)
   131→
   132→```
   133→Key:    session:{user_id}:{session_id}
   134→TTL:    30 days
   135→
   136→Value:  {
   137→  user_id:      "uuid",
   138→  session_id:   "uuid",
   139→  role:         "admin" | "member",
   140→  company_id:   "uuid" | null,
   141→  provider:     "github" | "google",
   142→  ip:           "x.x.x.x",
   143→  user_agent:   "...",
   144→  created_at:   "ISO timestamp",
   145→  last_active:  "ISO timestamp"
   146→}
   147→```
   148→
   149→### Session Operations
   150→
   151→- **Create** — on login, write to Redis with 30d TTL
   152→- **Validate** — on every request, auth guard checks key exists
   153→- **Refresh** — on token refresh, update `last_active`
   154→- **Destroy** — on logout, delete key
   155→- **Destroy all** — scan keys `session:{user_id}:*` and delete (or maintain a set `sessions:{user_id}` for O(1) lookup)
   156→
   157→### Company Context Switching (Members)
   158→
   159→- Member calls `POST /users/me/company` with `company_id`
   160→- Updates `company_id` in Redis session
   161→- Next access token includes new company context
   162→
   163→## API Endpoints
   164→
   165→### OAuth
   166→
   167→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   168→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   169→| GET    | /auth/github               | Redirect to GitHub OAuth       | Public   |
   170→| GET    | /auth/github/callback      | Handle GitHub callback         | Public   |
   171→| GET    | /auth/google               | Redirect to Google OAuth       | Public   |
   172→| GET    | /auth/google/callback      | Handle Google callback         | Public   |
   173→| POST   | /auth/refresh              | Refresh access token           | Cookie   |
   174→| POST   | /auth/logout               | Destroy session                | Auth     |
   175→| POST   | /auth/link                 | Link OAuth to existing user    | Auth     |
   176→
   177→### Signup
   178→
   179→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   180→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   181→| POST   | /auth/signup/company       | Create company after OAuth     | Auth     |
   182→
   183→### User
   184→
   185→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   186→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   187→| GET    | /users/me                  | Current user + active company  | Auth     |
   188→| POST   | /users/me/company          | Switch active company context  | Auth     |
   189→
   190→### Company
   191→
   192→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   193→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   194→| GET    | /companies/:id             | Company details                | Auth     |
   195→
   196→### Invitations (Admin only)
   197→
   198→| Method | Path                              | Description              | Auth     |
   199→|--------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
   200→| POST   | /companies/:id/invitations        | Invite engineer by email | Admin    |
   201→| GET    | /companies/:id/invitations        | List pending invitations | Admin    |
   202→| DELETE | /companies/:id/invitations/:invId | Revoke invitation        | Admin    |
   203→| GET    | /invitations/accept?token=xxx     | Accept invite → OAuth    | Public   |
   204→
   205→## Module Architecture
   206→
   207→```
   208→backend/src/modules/
   209→├── auth/
   210→│   ├── auth.module.ts
   211→│   ├── auth.controller.ts
   212→│   ├── auth.service.ts
   213→│   ├── strategies/
   214→│   │   ├── github.strategy.ts      # Arctic GitHub provider
   215→│   │   └── google.strategy.ts      # Arctic Google provider
   216→│   ├── guards/
   217→│   │   ├── auth.guard.ts           # JWT + session validation
   218→│   │   └── roles.guard.ts          # Role-based access
   219→│   ├── decorators/
   220→│   │   ├── current-user.decorator.ts
   221→│   │   ├── roles.decorator.ts
   222→│   │   └── public.decorator.ts
   223→│   ├── dto/
   224→│   ├── types/
   225→│   └── constants/
   226→├── session/
   227→│   ├── session.module.ts
   228→│   ├── session.service.ts
   229→│   └── types/
   230→├── user/
   231→│   ├── user.module.ts
   232→│   ├── user.service.ts
   233→│   ├── user.model.ts
   234→│   ├── dto/
   235→│   └── types/
   236→├── oauth-account/
   237→│   ├── oauth-account.module.ts
   238→│   ├── oauth-account.service.ts
   239→│   ├── oauth-account.model.ts
   240→│   ├── dto/
   241→│   └── types/
   242→├── company/
   243→│   ├── company.module.ts
   244→│   ├── company.service.ts
   245→│   ├── company.model.ts
   246→│   ├── dto/
   247→│   └── types/
   248→├── invitation/
   249→│   ├── invitation.module.ts
   250→│   ├── invitation.service.ts
   251→│   ├── invitation.model.ts
   252→│   ├── dto/
   253→│   └── types/
   254→├── cache/
   255→│   ├── cache.module.ts              # ioredis read/write + redlock
   256→│   └── cache.service.ts
   257→└── db/
   258→    └── db.module.ts                 # Knex pg read/write connections
   259→```
   260→
   261→## Security
   262→
   263→### OAuth
   264→
   265→- CSRF protection via `state` parameter (Arctic handles this)
   266→- PKCE for Google (Arctic native support)
   267→- OAuth access tokens encrypted at rest (AES-256) in `oauth_accounts.access_token`
   268→
   269→### JWT
   270→
   271→- Signed with HS256 (symmetric, single backend)
   272→- Access token payload: `{ user_id, session_id, role, company_id }` — minimal claims
   273→- Refresh token: opaque UUID stored alongside session in Redis (not a JWT)
   274→
   275→### Transport
   276→
   277→- Refresh token: `httpOnly`, `secure`, `sameSite: strict` cookie
   278→- Access token: response body, stored in memory on frontend (not localStorage)
   279→- CORS: restricted to frontend domain
   280→
   281→### Rate Limiting
   282→
   283→- `/auth/*` endpoints: 10 req/min per IP (`@nestjs/throttler`)
   284→- Token refresh: 5 req/min per user
   285→
   286→### Invitation Tokens
   287→
   288→- Cryptographically random (48 bytes, base64url)
   289→- 72hr expiry
   290→- Single use — `accepted_at` set on use
   291→
   292→## Infrastructure Dependencies
   293→
   294→### PostgreSQL
   295→
   296→- Write primary + read replica(s)
   297→- Knex.js via `nest-knexjs` with named connections (`KNEX_READ_CONNECTION`, `KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION`)
   298→- Client: `pg`
   299→
   300→### Redis
   301→
   302→- Write primary + read replica
   303→- ioredis with read/write separation (replicating recommendations-service pattern)
   304→- Keyv for cache abstraction
   305→- Redlock for distributed locking
   306→- Session TTL: 30 days
   307→
   308→### NPM Packages (New)
   309→
   310→- `arctic` — OAuth 2.0 provider helpers
   311→- `@nestjs/jwt` — JWT signing/verification
   312→- `pg` — PostgreSQL client
   313→- `knex` + `nest-knexjs` — query builder + NestJS integration
   314→- `ioredis` — Redis client
   315→- `@keyv/redis` — Keyv Redis adapter
   316→- `redlock` — distributed locking
   317→- `@nestjs/throttler` — rate limiting
   318→
   319→## Patterns (Following recommendations-service)
   320→
   321→### Database Layer
   322→
   323→- Knex read/write connection separation
   324→- Soft deletes (`.whereNull('deleted_at')` on all reads)
   325→- UUID primary keys via `randomUUID()` from `node:crypto`
   326→- Timestamps via `knex.fn.now()`
   327→- Input/output DTO validation via `plainToInstance()` + `validate()`
   328→- Thin service layer delegating to models
   329→- Table name constants (never raw strings)
   330→- Migrations wrapped in transactions, idempotent
   331→
   332→### Redis/Cache Layer
   333→
   334→- ioredis read/write clients with health-based fallback
   335→- Keyv cache managers with namespace isolation
   336→- Redlock for distributed locking
   337→- Recent-writes tracking for read-after-write consistency
   338→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Tools & Requirements
     6→
     7→- **Node.js**: >=v24.13.0 (see `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`)
     8→- **npm**: >=11.0.0
     9→- **Core stack**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript (strict mode)
    10→- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
    11→- **Code quality**: ESLint v9+ (flat config), Prettier, Husky
    12→
    13→## Key Commands
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Development
    17→npm run start:dev          # Hot reload via webpack
    18→npm run start:debug        # Debug mode with hot reload
    19→npm run build              # Production build
    20→npm run start:prod         # Run production build
    21→
    22→# Testing
    23→npm run test               # Full suite (lint, format, type-check, unit, e2e)
    24→npm run test:unit          # Unit tests only
    25→npm run test:e2e           # E2E tests only
    26→npm run test:coverage      # Full suite with coverage reports
    27→npm run test:unit:coverage # Unit tests with coverage
    28→npm run test:e2e:coverage  # E2E tests with coverage
    29→
    30→# Code Quality
    31→npm run code-quality-check # lint + format + type-check
    32→npm run lint               # Check linting
    33→npm run lint:fix           # Auto-fix linting issues
    34→npm run format             # Prettier format
    35→npm run type-check         # TypeScript check without emit
    36→```
    37→
    38→## Architecture
    39→
    40→NestJS microservice with Fastify adapter, strict TypeScript, and modular domain organization.
    41→
    42→### Project Structure
    43→
    44→```
    45→src/
    46→├── modules/
    47→│   ├── app/              # Root application module
    48→│   ├── health/           # Health check endpoints (liveness + readiness)
    49→│   └── env-config/       # Environment configuration & validation
    50→├── configs/              # Configuration files
    51→│   └── env-validations.config.ts  # class-validator decorators for env vars
    52→├── constants/            # Constants and enums
    53→│   └── env-variables.constant.ts  # Env variable name string exports
    54→├── dto/                  # Data Transfer Objects
    55→├── utils/                # Utility functions
    56→│   ├── common.util.ts           # Environment detection helpers
    57→│   ├── get-env-file.util.ts     # Env file loader & validator
    58→│   ├── get-env-variable.util.ts # getEnv standalone helper
    59→│   └── setup-swagger.util.ts    # Swagger/OpenAPI setup
    60→├── middlewares/          # Custom middlewares
    61→├── interfaces/           # TypeScript interfaces
    62→├── decorators/           # Custom decorators
    63→├── interceptors/         # NestJS interceptors
    64→├── guards/               # Auth guards
    65→└── unit-tests/           # Unit tests (*.unit-spec.ts)
    66→test/                     # E2E tests (*.e2e-spec.ts)
    67→eslint-rules/             # Custom ESLint rules
    68→└── require-object-params.js  # Enforces single object param rule
    69→```
    70→
    71→### Import Aliases (tsconfig.json)
    72→
    73→```typescript
    74→@config/*        → src/configs/*
    75→@constants/*     → src/constants/*
    76→@modules/*       → src/modules/*
    77→@utils/*         → src/utils/*
    78→@middlewares/*   → src/middlewares/*
    79→@services/*      → src/services/*
    80→@interfaces/*    → src/interfaces/*
    81→@decorators/*    → src/decorators/*
    82→@dto/*           → src/dto/*
    83→@guards/*        → src/guards/*
    84→@interceptors/*  → src/interceptors/*
    85→```
    86→
    87→## Development Rules
    88→
    89→### Code Style
    90→
    91→- **NO COMMENTS**, **NO EXPLANATIONS** in code (except ESLint disable commands)
    92→- **ALWAYS INSPECT EXISTING FILES** before creating new ones — match exact patterns:
    93→  - `.service.ts` → check similar modules
    94→  - `.controller.ts` → check `/src/modules/`
    95→  - `.dto.ts` → check `/src/dto/` or module folders
    96→  - `.util.ts` → check `/src/utils/`
    97→  - `.unit-spec.ts` → check `/src/unit-tests/`
    98→  - `.e2e-spec.ts` → check `/test/`
    99→  - `.interface.ts` → check `/src/interfaces/`
   100→- No `console.log` — use NestJS `Logger` instead
   101→- No relative imports — use `@aliases` only
   102→- All functions must have explicit return types
   103→- All custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter (enforced by custom ESLint rule `require-object-params`)
   104→
   105→### Environment Variables (CRITICAL)
   106→
   107→**ALWAYS follow this complete process when adding new environment variables:**
   108→
   109→#### Step 1: Add to Environment Files
   110→
   111→Add to all four env files:
   112→
   113→```bash
   114→# .env, .env.example
   115→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=default_value   # Description of what this variable does
   116→```
   117→
   118→```bash
   119→# .env.test, .env.test.example
   120→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=test_value
   121→```
   122→
   123→#### Step 2: Create Constant Export
   124→
   125→Add to `src/constants/env-variables.constant.ts`:
   126→
   127→```typescript
   128→export const NEW_VARIABLE_NAME = 'NEW_VARIABLE_NAME';
   129→```
   130→
   131→#### Step 3: Add Validation Rule
   132→
   133→Add to `src/configs/env-validations.config.ts` in the `EnvironmentVariables` class:
   134→
   135→```typescript
   136→@IsString()    // Use appropriate validator
   137→@IsNotEmpty()
   138→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: string;
   139→```
   140→
   141→**Common validators:** `@IsString()`, `@IsNumber()`, `@IsBoolean()`, `@IsEnum(EnumType)`, `@IsNotEmpty()`, `@Min()`, `@Max()`, `@IsUrl()`
   142→
   143→#### Step 4: Add to Environment Loader
   144→
   145→Add to `src/utils/get-env-file.util.ts` in `getEnvVariables()`:
   146→
   147→```typescript
   148→NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: process.env['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
   149→```
   150→
   151→#### Step 5: Add Test Coverage
   152→
   153→Add to `test/env-config.module.e2e-spec.ts`:
   154→
   155→```typescript
   156→// In beforeAll hook
   157→testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'] = String(envConfig['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME']);
   158→
   159→// Test case
   160→it('should load NEW_VARIABLE_NAME correctly', () => {
   161→  expect(envConfigService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME })).toBe(
   162→    testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
   163→  );
   164→});
   165→```
   166→
   167→#### Usage: Two Approaches
   168→
   169→**Approach 1: EnvConfigService (Dependency Injection)**
   170→
   171→```typescript
   172→import { EnvConfigService } from '@modules/env-config/env-config.service';
   173→import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';
   174→
   175→@Injectable()
   176→export class MyService {
   177→  constructor(private readonly configService: EnvConfigService) {}
   178→
   179→  someMethod(): void {
   180→    const value = this.configService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
   181→    const numericValue = this.configService.get<number>({
   182→      key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME,
   183→      converter: Number,
   184→    });
   185→  }
   186→}
   187→```
   188→
   189→**Approach 2: getEnv Utility (Standalone)**
   190→
   191→```typescript
   192→import { getEnv } from '@utils/get-env-variable.util';
   193→import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';
   194→
   195→const value = getEnv<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
   196→const numericValue = getEnv<number>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME, converter: Number });
   197→```
   198→
   199→**Important:**
   200→- **ALWAYS** complete ALL 5 steps — missing any step causes validation failures
   201→- **NEVER** access `process.env` directly in application code
   202→- **ALWAYS** use constants from `@constants/env-variables.constant`
   203→
   204→### Logging
   205→
   206→```typescript
   207→import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
   208→
   209→export class ClassName {
   210→  private readonly logger = new Logger(ClassName.name);
   211→}
   212→```
   213→
   214→### Common Patterns
   215→
   216→```typescript
   217→// Swagger decorators on controllers
   218→@ApiOperation({ summary: 'Description' })
   219→@ApiResponse({ status: HttpStatus.OK, description: 'Success' })
   220→```
   221→
   222→## ESLint Configuration
   223→
   224→- **No relative imports** (`no-restricted-imports` with `.*` pattern)
   225→- **No console** — use Logger
   226→- **Explicit return types** on all functions (`@typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types`)
   227→- **Import order**: builtin → external → internal (`@aliases`) → type, alphabetized, newline between groups
   228→- **Custom rule** `require-object-params`: all custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter
   229→- **Relaxed rules** in `*.spec.ts`, `test/**`, `src/decorators/**`, `src/middlewares/**`, `src/interceptors/**`, `src/guards/**`
   230→
   231→## TypeScript Configuration
   232→
   233→- Target ES2023, strict mode with all checks enabled
   234→- `strictPropertyInitialization: false` (NestJS DI pattern)
   235→- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, `noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature` enabled — be precise with types
   236→- Incremental compilation enabled for faster builds
   237→- Separate `tsconfig.test.json` relaxes strict checks for test files
   238→
   239→## Testing
   240→
   241→- **Unit**: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
   242→- **E2E**: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
   243→- **Coverage**: Separate reports in `coverage-unit/` and `coverage-e2e/`
   244→- Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
   245→- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements
   246→
   247→### Jest Config Files
   248→
   249→```
   250→test/
   251→├── jest.config.ts              # Shared base (moduleNameMapper, transform, preset)
   252→├── jest.coverage.config.ts     # Base + 95% thresholds
   253→├── jest-unit.config.ts         # Unit runner (testRegex: unit-spec.ts)
   254→├── jest-unit.coverage.config.ts # Unit + coverage enforcement
   255→├── jest-e2e.config.ts          # E2E runner (testRegex: e2e-spec.ts)
   256→└── jest-e2e.coverage.config.ts  # E2E + coverage enforcement (controllers + services only)
   257→```
   258→
   259→### Unit Test Patterns
   260→
   261→```typescript
   262→// Service test — mock dependencies via Test.createTestingModule
   263→const module: TestingModule = await Test.createTestingModule({
   264→  providers: [
   265→    MyService,
   266→    { provide: MyDependency, useValue: mockDependency },
   267→  ],
   268→}).compile();
   269→service = module.get<MyService>(MyService);
   270→
   271→// Spy on Logger to prevent console noise and assert calls
   272→loggerSpy = jest.spyOn(Logger.prototype, 'log').mockImplementation(jest.fn());
   273→
   274→// Always clear mocks between tests
   275→afterEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks());
   276→```
   277→
   278→### E2E Test Patterns
   279→
   280→```typescript
   281→// HTTP testing — let TerminusModule run the real health check chain
   282→const moduleFixture = await Test.createTestingModule({
   283→  imports: [TerminusModule.forRoot({ gracefulShutdownTimeoutMs: 0, errorLogStyle: 'json' })],
   284→  controllers: [MyController],
   285→  providers: [MyService, { provide: Dependency, useValue: mock }],
   286→}).compile();
   287→
   288→const app = moduleFixture.createNestApplication();
   289→const httpServer = app.getHttpServer() as App;
   290→await app.init();
   291→
   292→// Use supertest for HTTP assertions
   293→await request(httpServer).get('/endpoint').expect(200);
   294→```
   295→
   296→### Coverage Requirements
   297→
   298→- **95% minimum** on branches, functions, lines, statements — enforced by Jest, CI fails if not met
   299→- Unit coverage collects from all `src/**/*.ts` (excluding `main.ts`, modules, interfaces, constants, test files)
   300→- E2E coverage collects only from `*.(controller|service).ts` files
   301→
   302→## Key Dependencies
   303→
   304→### Core
   305→
   306→- **@nestjs/\*** — NestJS framework and modules
   307→- **@nestjs/platform-fastify** — Fastify adapter for performance
   308→- **@nestjs/terminus** — Health check module
   309→- **@nestjs/swagger** — OpenAPI/Swagger documentation
   310→
   311→### Validation & Transform
   312→
   313→- **class-validator** + **class-transformer** — DTO validation and transformation
   314→
   315→### Utilities
   316→
   317→- **rxjs** — Reactive patterns
   318→- **axios** / **@nestjs/axios** — HTTP requests
   319→

Now I have a thorough understanding of the design spec and the existing codebase context. Here is my review.

---

# Auth Design Spec Review

**File reviewed:** `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-22-auth-design.md`

---

## What is done well

- Clean separation of concerns across modules (auth, session, user, company, invitation, cache, db).
- Token exchange pattern (temporary auth code from callback, not raw JWTs in the URL) is a solid security choice.
- Refresh token rotation with opaque UUIDs stored in Redis is correct practice.
- Session-backed JWT validation (checking Redis on every request) gives immediate revocation capability, which is critical for B2B.
- The data model is normalized sensibly: `oauth_accounts` separated from `users`, `company_members` as a join table.
- Following existing recommendations-service patterns for Knex read/write separation and ioredis is good for consistency.
- Invitation tokens: 48-byte cryptographically random, 72hr expiry, single-use -- all sound.

---

## CRITICAL

### C1. Dual/conflicting role and company modeling on `users` table

The `users` table has both a `role` column (admin/member) and a `company_id` FK. Simultaneously, `company_members` has its own `role` column per membership. This creates ambiguity:

- Which `role` is authoritative -- `users.role` or `company_members.role`?
- The spec says `company_id` on `users` is "the admin's owned company, NULL for pure members." But the session stores a single `role` and `company_id`, and members can switch companies via `POST /users/me/company`. When a member switches companies, what role do they have? The `users.role` says "member", but nothing stops the `company_members.role` from differing.
- If a user is an admin of Company A and gets invited as a member to Company B (the spec says admins are 1:1 with a company, but there is no DB constraint preventing an admin from also appearing in `company_members` for another company), the `users.role` would say "admin" while their `company_members` entry for Company B says "member."

**Recommendation:** Remove `role` and `company_id` from the `users` table entirely. Derive role from `company_members` based on the active company context. The session already stores `company_id` and `role` -- these should come from `company_members` at login/context-switch time, not from a static column on `users`. This eliminates the single source of truth problem.

### C2. No database-level constraint for admin 1:1 company enforcement

The spec states "Admins: 1:1 with company (enforced in app logic)." App-logic-only enforcement of a critical invariant in a multi-tenant system is dangerous. Concurrent requests or bugs in the service layer could create multiple admins per company or one admin across multiple companies.

**Recommendation:** Add a partial unique index on `company_members`: `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON company_members(company_id) WHERE role = 'admin' AND deleted_at IS NULL`. This guarantees at the database level that each company has at most one admin. Also consider using Redlock during admin assignment as a belt-and-suspenders approach.

### C3. Token exchange flow is underspecified and potentially broken

The spec describes two contradictory flows:

1. Section "OAuth Login Sequence" step 8 says: "Return JWT (access + refresh) to frontend."
2. Section "Token Exchange Security" says: "OAuth callback returns a temporary authorization code to frontend (not JWT). Frontend exchanges code for JWT via POST."

But there is no `POST /auth/token` or `POST /auth/exchange` endpoint in the API table. The callback endpoints (`GET /auth/{provider}/callback`) are listed, but no exchange endpoint exists. The actual mechanism for the frontend to receive tokens is undefined.

**Recommendation:** Add a `POST /auth/token` (or `POST /auth/exchange`) endpoint to the API table. Clarify the full sequence: callback stores a short-lived code in Redis, redirects to frontend with the code as a query parameter, frontend POSTs the code to the exchange endpoint, backend returns access token in body and sets refresh token as httpOnly cookie.

---

## MAJOR

### M1. Refresh token storage location is not defined

The spec says refresh tokens are "opaque UUID stored alongside session in Redis" but the session structure does not include a `refresh_token` field. Where exactly is the refresh token stored? Is it a separate Redis key? Is it a field in the session hash? The refresh flow says "validates refresh token, checks session exists" but does not explain the mapping between a refresh token and a session.

**Recommendation:** Either add `refresh_token` to the session structure, or define a separate Redis key pattern like `refresh:{token_uuid} -> session:{user_id}:{session_id}`. Also specify that on rotation, the old refresh token must be invalidated atomically (using a Redis transaction or Redlock) to prevent race conditions with concurrent refresh requests.

### M2. Account linking flow is vague and has no resolution endpoint

Step 6 of the OAuth flow mentions "prompt to link" for two cases, but the actual UX/API flow is unclear:

- What does "prompt to link" mean in a server-side OAuth callback? The backend cannot show a UI prompt.
- `POST /auth/link` exists but requires Auth -- the user is not yet authenticated at this point in the flow (they are trying to log in via a new provider).
- How does the backend communicate to the frontend that linking is needed? What temporary state is stored? How does the frontend confirm the link?

**Recommendation:** Define the linking flow explicitly. For example: backend detects the linking case, stores a temporary linking token in Redis, redirects to frontend with a linking prompt URL. The user then authenticates with their existing provider, and the frontend calls `POST /auth/link` with the linking token + existing session. Alternatively, auto-link when the email matches (simpler, but has security implications if email is not verified by the provider).

### M3. `invitations.role` allows admin invitations, contradicting the admin model

The `invitations` table has `role: ENUM(admin, member)`. But the spec says admins create companies themselves via `POST /auth/signup/company` and have a 1:1 relationship. If you can invite someone as an admin, you would have two admins for one company, or you would be transferring ownership -- neither of which is described.

**Recommendation:** Either restrict `invitations.role` to `member` only, or define an explicit ownership transfer flow. If the intent is future flexibility, document it as such but implement the constraint now.

### M4. No PKCE for GitHub OAuth

The spec calls out "PKCE for Google (Arctic native support)" but does not mention PKCE for GitHub. GitHub has supported PKCE since 2021. Without PKCE, the GitHub flow is vulnerable to authorization code interception attacks, especially on mobile or when the redirect URI is not perfectly locked down.

**Recommendation:** Enable PKCE for GitHub as well. Arctic supports it.

### M5. Missing `invited_by` on invitations table

There is no record of which admin created an invitation. This is important for audit trails in a B2B app and for debugging when customers report issues.

**Recommendation:** Add `invited_by UUID FK -> users._id NOT NULL` to the `invitations` table.

### M6. No "logout all sessions" API endpoint

The session operations section describes "Destroy all" (scan/delete all sessions for a user), but there is no corresponding API endpoint. This is an important security feature for B2B (e.g., when an employee leaves or credentials are compromised).

**Recommendation:** Add `POST /auth/logout/all` to the API table.

---

## MINOR

### m1. Session `company_id` for admins is redundant but could desync

For admins, `company_id` is derivable from the user record (or from `company_members`). Storing it in the session creates a cache that could become stale if, for example, company deletion happens but sessions are not invalidated. Members have the same risk when they are removed from a company.

**Recommendation:** Document the invalidation strategy: when a user is removed from a company or a company is deleted, all relevant sessions must be destroyed. Consider adding a note about this to the "Destroy all" session operation.

### m2. `GET /companies/:id` authorization is too broad

The endpoint is listed as "Auth" (any authenticated user). In a multi-tenant B2B app, a user should only be able to view companies they belong to.

**Recommendation:** Add authorization: the user must be a member of the company (check `company_members`), or restrict to the user's active company context.

### m3. Rate limiting for invitation endpoints is not specified

`/auth/*` has rate limits, but invitation creation (`POST /companies/:id/invitations`) does not. An admin could accidentally or maliciously spam invitations.

**Recommendation:** Add rate limiting for invitation creation (e.g., 20 per hour per company).

### m4. No `UNIQUE(company_id, email)` on invitations

The same email could be invited to the same company multiple times (creating duplicate pending invitations), which would confuse admins and waste invitation tokens.

**Recommendation:** Add a unique constraint on `(company_id, email)` where `accepted_at IS NULL AND deleted_at IS NULL`.

### m5. `oauth_accounts.email` purpose is unclear

The `oauth_accounts` table has an `email` field separate from `users.email`. It is not clear when these would differ or how conflicts are resolved. If the user changes their email at the OAuth provider, which email is canonical?

**Recommendation:** Add a note clarifying that `oauth_accounts.email` captures the provider-reported email at link time, `users.email` is the canonical app email, and describe whether/how provider email changes are synced.

### m6. HS256 JWT signing

HS256 (symmetric) works for a single backend, as noted. However, if the architecture ever scales to multiple services that need to verify tokens (e.g., a separate websocket service, a frontend SSR server), HS256 requires sharing the secret with all verifiers.

**Recommendation:** This is fine for now but note it as a future consideration. If multi-service verification is anticipated, RS256/ES256 with a public key would be more appropriate.

---

## SUGGESTIONS

### S1. Consider a `users_companies` view or helper

Since the role/company relationship should be derived from `company_members`, consider creating a database view or a Knex query helper that joins `users` with `company_members` for the active company. This will be reused heavily.

### S2. Add `last_login_at` to users

Useful for admin dashboards and security auditing. Can be updated on session creation.

### S3. Define the signup flow for invited members more explicitly

The spec has `GET /invitations/accept?token=xxx` which "Accept invite -> OAuth", but the full flow is unclear. Does accepting an invitation redirect to OAuth? What if the user already has an account? What if they authenticate with a different email than the invitation was sent to? Spell out the branching logic.

### S4. Consider refresh token family/lineage tracking

When rotating refresh tokens, if a previously-rotated (now-invalid) refresh token is reused, it likely indicates token theft. A common pattern is to track refresh token families and invalidate the entire family (all sessions) when reuse is detected.

### S5. Module architecture does not show a `company-member` module

The `company_members` table is in the data model but there is no corresponding module in the architecture tree. Clarify whether `company.service.ts` owns the membership CRUD or if a separate module is planned.

---

## Summary

The design is well-structured overall and makes solid choices on token handling, session management, and multi-provider OAuth. The three critical issues -- the dual role/company modeling ambiguity (C1), lack of DB-level admin uniqueness enforcement (C2), and the underspecified token exchange flow (C3) -- should be resolved before implementation begins, as they affect the core data model and primary authentication flow. The major issues (M1-M6) are important for security and completeness but can be addressed during implementation if needed.

Review the design spec at /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-22-auth-design.md

This is an authentication and authorization design for a multi-tenant B2B NestJS app. Check for:

1. **Completeness** — are there gaps in the auth flows, edge cases not covered?
2. **Consistency** — do the data model, API endpoints, and session design align?
3. **Security** — any vulnerabilities or missing security measures?
4. **Feasibility** — any design decisions that would be hard to implement or maintain?
5. **Contradictions** — anything that conflicts with itself?

Context:
- NestJS + Fastify backend, React frontend
- OAuth via Arctic (GitHub + Google)
- JWT access (1hr) + opaque refresh token (30d)
- Sessions in Redis with read/write separation
- PostgreSQL with read/write replicas via Knex.js
- Multi-tenant: companies with admin (1:1) and member (many:many) roles
- Follows patterns from an existing recommendations-service (Knex, ioredis, redlock)

Report issues categorized as Critical / Major / Minor / Suggestions.

Useful for admin dashboards and security auditing. Can be updated on session creation.

### S3. Define the signup flow for invited members more explicitly

The spec has `GET /invitations/accept?token=xxx` which "Accept invite -> OAuth", but the full flow is unclear. Does accepting an invitation redirect to OAuth? What if the user already has an account? What if they authenticate with a different email than the invitation was sent to? Spell out the branching logic.

### S4. Consider refresh token family/lineage tracking

When rotating refresh tokens, if a previously-rotated (now-invalid) refresh token is reused, it likely indicates token theft. A common pattern is to track refresh token families and invalidate the entire family (all sessions) when reuse is detected.

### S5. Module architecture does not show a `company-member` module

The `company_members` table is in the data model but there is no corresponding module in the architecture tree. Clarify whether `company.service.ts` owns the membership CRUD or if a separate module is planned.

---

## Summary

The design is well-structured overall and makes solid choices on token handling, session management, and multi-provider OAuth. The three critical issues -- the dual role/company modeling ambiguity (C1), lack of DB-level admin uniqueness enforcement (C2), and the underspecified token exchange flow (C3) -- should be resolved before implementation begins, as they affect the core data model and primary authentication flow. The major issues (M1-M6) are important for security and completeness but can be addressed during implementation if needed.

Good review. Found real issues. Let me fix the spec.

**Critical fixes needed:**

1. **C1** — Remove `role` and `company_id` from `users` table. Derive from `company_members` based on active company context.
2. **C2** — Add partial unique index: one admin per company at DB level.
3. **C3** — Add `POST /auth/token` exchange endpoint, clarify the full callback → exchange flow.

**Major fixes:**

4. **M1** — Define refresh token storage: separate Redis key `refresh:{token} → session:{user_id}:{session_id}`
5. **M2** — Spell out the linking flow with temporary linking token
6. **M3** — Restrict `invitations.role` to `member` only for now
7. **M4** — PKCE for GitHub too
8. **M5** — Add `invited_by` to invitations
9. **M6** — Add `POST /auth/logout/all` endpoint

**Minor fixes:** m1-m6 and suggestions S1-S5 — all valid, incorporating.

Updating the spec now.

   118→
   119→### Request Authentication
   120→
   121→On every authenticated request:
   122→1. Validate JWT signature + expiry
   123→2. Check session exists in Redis (key: `session:{user_id}:{session_id}`)
   124→3. If session gone → 401, force re-login
   125→
   126→### Logout
   127→
   128→- Delete session key from Redis → JWT becomes invalid immediately
   129→
   130→## Session Structure (Redis)
   131→
   132→```
   133→Key:    session:{user_id}:{session_id}
   134→TTL:    30 days
   135→
   136→Value:  {
   137→  user_id:      "uuid",
   138→  session_id:   "uuid",

   145→  last_active:  "ISO timestamp"

   148→
   149→### Session Operations
   150→
   151→- **Create** — on login, write to Redis with 30d TTL
   152→- **Validate** — on every request, auth guard checks key exists
   153→- **Refresh** — on token refresh, update `last_active`
   154→- **Destroy** — on logout, delete key
   155→- **Destroy all** — scan keys `session:{user_id}:*` and delete (or maintain a set `sessions:{user_id}` for O(1) lookup)
   156→
   157→### Company Context Switching (Members)
   158→
   159→- Member calls `POST /users/me/company` with `company_id`

   162→

   164→

   168→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|

   171→| GET    | /auth/google               | Redirect to Google OAuth       | Public   |
   172→| GET    | /auth/google/callback      | Handle Google callback         | Public   |
   173→| POST   | /auth/refresh              | Refresh access token           | Cookie   |
   174→| POST   | /auth/logout               | Destroy session                | Auth     |
   175→| POST   | /auth/link                 | Link OAuth to existing user    | Auth     |
   176→
   177→### Signup
   178→
   179→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   180→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   181→| POST   | /auth/signup/company       | Create company after OAuth     | Auth     |
   182→
   183→### User
   184→
   185→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   186→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   187→| GET    | /users/me                  | Current user + active company  | Auth     |
   188→| POST   | /users/me/company          | Switch active company context  | Auth     |
   189→
   190→### Company
   191→
   192→| Method | Path                       | Description                    | Auth     |
   193→|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
   194→| GET    | /companies/:id             | Company details                | Auth     |
   195→
   196→### Invitations (Admin only)
   197→
   198→| Method | Path                              | Description              | Auth     |
   199→|--------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
   200→| POST   | /companies/:id/invitations        | Invite engineer by email | Admin    |
   201→| GET    | /companies/:id/invitations        | List pending invitations | Admin    |
